mfa.reaction_or_metabolite: central-carbon fluxes (glycolysis, PPP, TCA, glyoxylate, anaplerotic, acetate)
mfa.note: Fig 3A/3B compare relative flux ranges from carbon-13 MFA in the Keio database vs GSMN flux variability analysis; values plotted relative to GLCptspp, range = mean +/- 2 SD of 4 replicates. No absolute mmol/gDW/h numbers appear in the main text.

growth_records.cite: Mannan et al. Integrating Kinetic Model of E. coli with Genome Scale Metabolic Fluxes Overcomes Its Open System Problem and Reveals Bistability in Central Metabolism. PLoS ONE (2015). DOI:10.1371/journal.pone.0139507. [PMC4607504]
growth_records.snippet: we turned to the multi-omics data from the Keio database, for growth rate 0.2 h-1. In particular we focused on the reported fluxomics data, which were experimentally estimated from carbon-13 metabolic flux analysis ... our model represents
growth_records.notes: Modeling paper (E. coli central-carbon kinetic model + iAF1260 GSMN). The ONLY experimental prokaryote physiology it uses is the Keio multi-omics steady-state dataset (refs 15,16 = Keio database; C13-MFA = ref 28), for a fixed growth rate of 0.2 h^-1, aerobic, 37C, pH 7.0, glucose sole carbon source. These uptake/secretion/central-carbon fluxes are CITED from the Keio database (Ishii et al. multi-omics), NOT measured in this paper, and are presented in Fig 3 only relative to the GLCptspp reaction (mean +/- 2 SD of 4 replicates), so no absolute mmol/gDW/h rate could be extracted without inventing numbers. doubling_time_h = ln2/mu = 0.6931/0.2 = 3.466 h (mu=D at steady state). Record retained only as a pointer to the underlying Keio experimental condition; all is_experimental flags set false because rates here are secondary/cited and lack absolute values.

growth_records.snippet: The best-performing isolate ... exhibited a doubling time of 4.3 ± 0.1 h (mean ± s.d.) ... This new reference strain, MEcoli_ref_2 ... was used for all further experiments.
growth_records.notes: Measured doubling time Td=4.3±0.1 h in M9+500 mM methanol, 37 C, aerobic (microplate). mu computed: mu=ln2/Td=0.6931/4.3=0.161 /h (growth_method=calculated). No specific substrate uptake or product secretion measured for the base strain. Methanol evaporation ~2-3 mM/h at 37 C stated as a caveat, not an uptake rate.

growth_records.snippet: MEcoli_ref_2 grew exponentially to an OD600 of 100.2 (22.1 ± 0.5 g CDW l−1 (mean ± s.d.; CDW, cell dry weight)) at a doubling time of 4.9 h. At the achieved biomass concentration, oxygen transfer became limiting.

growth_records.snippet: Growth and induction of the ldh-expressing strain in minimal medium supplemented with 500 mM methanol resulted in a maximum titre of 284.0 ± 23.6 mg l−1 (3.2 ± 0.3 mM) for lactic acid with a carbon yield of 15.7 ± 1.0% (0.147 ± 0.009 g lact
growth_records.notes: Measured secretion of lactic acid from methanol: titre 284.0±23.6 mg/L (=3.2±0.3 mM) at 22 h; volumetric productivity 12.7±1.0 mg/L/h; carbon yield 15.7±1.0% (mass yield 0.147±0.009 g/g MeOH). 13C-methanol labelling confirmed 97% product labelling (product genuinely from methanol). Units left volumetric (mg/L/h) as reported; conversion to mmol/gDW/h not possible without biomass time-course. No growth rate reported for this plasmid-bearing condition.

up.method: Specific glucose uptake rate (GUR) = growth rate / biomass yield; glucose concentration measured with YSI 2700 biochemistry analyzer; numeric per-strain GUR values reported only in S1 File (supplementary), not in the article full text
Specific oxygen uptake rate (OUR) computed from electron balance OUR = 24*GUR - 8*APR - 4.35/0.0255*GR (degree of reduction 4.35, 0.0255 gDW/mmol-C); numeric values in S1 File, not in full text

sec.method: Specific acetate production rate (APR) = specific glucose uptake rate * acetate yield; acetate measured by HPLC; only glucose and acetate detected as extracellular compounds; numeric per-strain APR values in S1 File, not in full text

yields.Yxs: biomass yield on glucose determined as slope of biomass conc vs glucose conc regression (numeric value in S1 File)

product_yields.acetate_yield: from initial and final glucose and acetate concentrations (numeric value in S1 File)

growth_records.snippet: whose knockout does not alter growth rate (0.73 +/- 0.01 hr-1 13C-glucose vs. 0.73 +/- 0.02 hr-1 12C-glucose, biological triplicates)... cultures were serially propagated in 15 mL flasks of M9 minimal medium with 2 g/L 13C-glucose, kept at
growth_records.notes: Wild-type/ancestor E. coli K-12 MG1655 on [U-13C]glucose. Growth rate 0.73 +/- 0.01 h-1 explicitly reported (biological triplicate). doubling_time = ln2/mu = 0.6931/0.73 = 0.9495 h. Aerobic batch (M9 + 2 g/L glucose, 37C). Glucose uptake (YSI), acetate secretion (HPLC), and O2 uptake (electron-balance calc) were all MEASURED/derived, but the numeric per-strain rate values (GUR, APR, OUR) and yields (Yxs, acetate yield) are reported only in S1 File supplementary, which is not part of the harvested full-text XML; rates left null rather than invented. Formulas stated in Methods: GUR = GR/Yxs; APR = GUR*acetate_yield; OUR = 24*GUR - 8*APR - (4.35/0.0255)*GR.

growth_records.snippet: The strain delta-frmA delta-fbp (n=1) was evolved for 38 generations with a generation time of 25 h in the first 7 generations, 20 h for the next 6 generations and 15 h for the remaining generations.
growth_records.notes: Chemostat adaptive evolution; growth improved over time. Generation (doubling) times reported: 25 h (gen 1-7), 20 h (gen 8-13), 15 h (thereafter). mu = ln2/td: 0.693/25=0.028 h-1 (initial), 0.693/20=0.035 h-1, 0.693/15=0.046 h-1 (evolved). Reported representative = initial td 25 h / mu 0.028 h-1; evolved reaches mu ~0.046 h-1. No uptake/secretion rates measured. pH 7.0 (1M NH3 / 1M H2SO4 control).

growth_records.notes: Fully-defined prokaryote batch growth condition; medium composition explicitly stated. E. coli used only as a factory to make 13C isotopologue standards on acetate as sole C source; growth monitored by OD600 (biomass conversion 0.37 gDW/OD) but NO growth rate, uptake or secretion rate is reported, so all rate fields null/empty. The 13C-MFA fluxes elsewhere in this paper are for Brassica napus leaves (a plant/eukaryote, U-13C-pyruvate labeling of leaf discs) and are therefore EXCLUDED from prokaryote records. Recorded here for its GEM-relevant defined acetate minimal-medium condition.

Aerobic fed-batch in 10 L stirred bioreactor at 35C. Batch: 22 g/L glucose, 9 h, reached 10 gDCW/L. Yxs CALCULATED = 10 gDCW/L / 22 g glucose/L = 0.45 gDCW/g (assumes glucose fully consumed at batch end and negligible inoculum biomass). Specific substrate uptake rate qs (g/g/h) was varied across cultivations and shown only graphically in Figure 1G/1H (rod->spherical SF vs qs), no numeric qs in text. Induction one-point IPTG pulse to 0.5 mM. pH not stated. Products (GFP+, N-pro) accumulate as intracellular inclusion bodies -> no extracellular secretion rates.

growth_records.snippet: The dilution rate (setting the generation time) was set to four hours during the first month and two hours thereafter. ... the value for the growth rate at equilibrium is the dilution rate like in a classical chemostat.
growth_records.notes: Thymine-limited glucose chemostat of E. coli β7328. Generation (doubling) time set to 4 h during the first month. In a chemostat at steady state µ=D and D=ln2/td, so µ=D=ln2/4 h=0.6931/4=0.1733 h⁻¹; doubling_time_h=4 (as stated). No substrate uptake or product secretion rates were measured in this paper (device/experimental-evolution study). pH not stated. Glucose stated as 10 mM in Results (chemostat inoculum in MS medium); Methods notes routine MS medium with 2 g glucose/L for plates/batch. Thymine 1 µM (growth-limiting); 5 µM stated as minimum for optimal proliferation, 10 µM used as excess for batch max-growth-rate measurements (numeric µ not given in text, only Fig 4). MS base salts composition given only by reference [24], not in text.

growth_records.strain: β7328 (ΔthyA ΔdeoCABD p::deoA+; thyA::erm+, overproducing thymidine phosphorylase) and evolved descendants (γ1710–γ8094) over ~10,000 generations
growth_records.cite: de Crécy-Lagard et al. Long term adaptation of a microbial population to a permanent metabolic constraint: overcoming thymineless death by experimental evolution of Escherichia coli. BMC Biotechnology (2001). DOI:10.1186/1472-6750-1-10. [PMC60676]
growth_records.snippet: one nutrient flow reversal and one sterilization phase per 48 h for a 2-h generation time. ... the value for the growth rate at equilibrium is the dilution rate like in a classical chemostat.
growth_records.notes: Main long-term thymine-limited glucose chemostat condition of E. coli β7328 (generation time = 2 h, set from second month for the remainder of the 880-day experiment). Chemostat steady state: µ=D=ln2/td=0.6931/2 h=0.3466 h⁻¹; doubling_time_h=2 (as stated). No measured substrate uptake or product secretion rates reported (evolution/device paper). Biomass at plateau OD600≈1.1 ≈ 5×10^8 cells/mL (stated OD 1 corresponds to 5×10^8 cells/mL). pH not stated. Thymine 1 µM limiting; glucose 10 mM (Results). MS salts composition only via reference [24].

growth_records.cite: Lin et al. Metabolic engineering of Escherichia coli for the production of riboflavin. Microbial Cell Factories (2014). DOI:10.1186/s12934-014-0104-5. [PMC4223517]
growth_records.snippet: A maximal riboflavin production reached 2702.8±89.9 mg/L using the optimized medium with a yield of 137.5 mg riboflavin/g glucose in shake flask cultivation ... residual glucose was almost exhausted after 60 h culture
growth_records.notes: Best overall condition. Yield 137.5 mg/g (0.138 g/g). Glucose consumed computed = 2702.8/137.5 = 19.66 g/L, implying optimized MS medium held ~20 g/L glucose (higher than the 10 g/L of other conditions; exact composition in Additional file Table S2, not in main text). Glycine addition boosted riboflavin (purine precursor -> GTP). ~60 h; glucose nearly exhausted. Biomass not tabulated for optimized run so no specific rate. Note: paper contrasts with industrial fed-batch producers (up to 20 g/L RF, 20.7 mg/gCDW/h) which are cited FROM refs 5,13,47 (not measured here).

growth_records.snippet: We fed at a constant specific glucose uptake rate (qs,glu) of 0.25 g/g/h... a constant biomass yield (YX/S = 0.37 g/g...). GFP: qs,lac,max* 0.23, qs,glu,crit 0.88, qs,lac,noglu 0.023 g/g/h (Table 1).
growth_records.notes: E. coli BL21(DE3)/pET21a(+) producing cytoplasmic GFP. Base salt composition NOT given in paper (cited DeLisa et al. ref 35) so medium_composition_stated=false; only feeds/antibiotic/batch glucose given. Growth rate CALCULATED: mu = qs,glu * Yx/s = 0.25 g/g/h * 0.37 g/g = 0.0925 /h; doubling time = ln2/mu = 0.693/0.0925 = 7.49 h (applies to the qs,glu=0.25 biomass fed-batch phase common to all four strains). qs,glu setpoint controlled via feed. Unit conversions (mmol/gDW/h): glucose 0.25 g/g/h /180.16*1000 = 1.39; qs,glu,crit 0.88 -> 4.88; lactose (MW 342.3) qs,lac,max* 0.23 -> 0.672; qs,lac,noglu 0.023 -> 0.067. Batch phase at 35 C (biomass 8-9 g DCW/L), induction phase at 30 C (temperature_C=30 reported here). Glucose accumulation observed above qs,glu ~0.32 g/g/h. qs,lac,max* and qs,glu,crit are mechanistic-model fits to experimental lactose-uptake measurements.

growth_records.notes: Doubling time reported directly as 64 min by OD600 (also 65 min = ln(2)/0.0106 min-1 from 15N NanoSIMS population fit; the two agree). Growth rate computed mu = ln2/td = 0.6931/(64/60 h) = 0.6931/1.0667 h = 0.650 /h. doubling_time_h = 64/60 = 1.067 h. Batch flask culture, aerobic (240 rpm), 37°C. pH not stated. No substrate uptake, product secretion, or MFA fluxes reported (this is a 15N single-cell SIMS growth-heterogeneity study). Media composition fully stated per liter as above.

up.method: GC-EI-QTOF-MS absolute quantification of supernatant glucose over growth time-course

mfa.units: relative (all central-carbon fluxes normalized to glucose uptake = 100)
mfa.note: 13C-MFA via INCA/EMU; absolute fluxes and CIs in Supplementary Data A5; flux map in Fig. 2 (glycolysis, PPP, TCA, ED pathway, glyoxylate shunt, anaplerotic). CI reductions with tandem-MS: glycolysis ~28%, TCA up to 67%, anaplerotic/glyoxylate ~65%.

growth_records.cite: Richter C et al. Improved 13C metabolic flux analysis in Escherichia coli metabolism: application of a high-resolution MS (GC-EI-QTOF) for comprehensive assessment of MS/MS fragments. Journal of Industrial Microbiology & Biotechnology (2023). DOI:10.1093/jimb/kuad039. [PMC10716738]
growth_records.method: experimentally measured (specific growth rate determined from OD600/CDW time-course on unlabeled glucose) but numeric value only in Supplementary Data A5, not in body text
growth_records.snippet: Glucose uptake, acetate excretion, and the specific growth rate were determined in cultures grown on M9 medium supplemented with unlabeled ([12C]) glucose... Metabolic flux distributions of E. coli grown in aerobic batch culture... normaliz
growth_records.notes: Genuine prokaryote (E. coli DH5-alpha) aerobic 13C-MFA batch on M9 + 11 mmol/L glucose, 37C, 140 rpm, mid-exponential. Glucose uptake, acetate excretion and mu WERE experimentally measured (GC-EI-QTOF for glucose, HPLC for acetate, OD/CDW for mu) but their ABSOLUTE numeric values, plus the full absolute MFA flux distribution, are reported only in Supplementary Data A5 (external XLSX) - NOT in the article XML body. Body only states fluxes are normalized to glucose uptake=100 (Fig. 2). No numeric rates invented. Medium composition beyond glucose is by reference to Wilms et al. 2001 (not listed), hence medium_composition_stated:false.

growth_records.method: reported (endpoint OD600 4.58 +/- 0.18 at 48 h); specific growth rate not cleanly derivable because growth was non-exponential and stopped after ~24 h, and only endpoint OD is given numerically (time-course only in figures).
growth_records.snippet: TA2463 produced 37.7 +/- 1.6 mM 3-HP and the OD600 and glycerol consumption at 48 h were 4.58 +/- 0.18, 163.9 +/- 1.8 mM, respectively. The 3-HP yield from glycerol was 26.5 +/- 1.1 % (mol/mol). A significant amount of 1,3-propanediol (12.8
growth_records.notes: Reference synthetic-pathway strain (no MTS repression), IPTG 0.1 mM added at 0 h. Measured at 48 h: 3-HP 37.7+/-1.6 mM, 1,3-PDO 12.8+/-1.8 mM, glycerol consumed 163.9+/-1.8 mM, OD600 4.58+/-0.18, acetate max 2.18 mM (24 h, then reassimilated), lactate not detected. Yield reported 26.5+/-1.1 % mol 3-HP/mol glycerol; my cross-check 37.7/163.9 = 0.230 (23.0%) differs from stated 26.5% (authors likely referenced glycerol consumed at a different sampling point) - stated value retained. Volumetric rates derived by dividing endpoint titer/consumption by 48 h (batch runtime). qS in mmol/gDW/h NOT computable: no OD600-to-gDW conversion factor given. Not a 13C-MFA paper; authors state 13C-MFA is future work; LC-MS gave only relative (fold-change) intracellular metabolite levels, not fluxes.

growth_records.snippet: In defined minimal medium, the untreated control exhibited a maximal specific growth rate (mumax) of ~0.23 h-1 and the highest carrying capacities of the conditions tested (OD600 ~2.2)
growth_records.notes: Only the untreated control is a clean unperturbed prokaryote growth-physiology record; all other conditions are CORM/antibiotic-inhibited and not model-building physiology. mu_max reported directly as ~0.23 h-1 (max slope of ln(OD600) vs t). Doubling time calculated: td = ln2/mu = 0.693/0.23 = 3.01 h. No substrate uptake or product secretion rates were measured in this paper. Medium is a defined minimal medium (ref [18]) with 5% glycerol; full salt composition not stated in text so medium_composition_stated=false. A separate checkerboard assay used defined minimal medium with 5 g/L glucose (OD600 0.3) but no growth rate was reported for that carbon source.

up.method: derived from chemostat mass balance qS=D*(Sfeed-Sresidual)/X

yields.Yxs: biomass ~4 g/L from feed glucose 8 g/L => ~0.5 gDW/g glucose (derived)

growth_records.cite: Li Zhaopeng et al. Is energy excess the initial trigger of carbon overflow metabolism? Transcriptional network response of carbon-limited Escherichia coli to transient carbon excess. Microbial Cell Factories (2022). DOI:10.1186/s12934-022-01787-4. [PMC9027384]
growth_records.snippet: Slow-growing cells at steady state in a carbon-limited continuous culture (D = mu = 0.072 h-1, biomass concentration approx. 4 g L-1)... The pH was kept at pH 6.6 and the dissolved oxygen concentration (pO2) was always above 40% air saturat
growth_records.notes: Steady-state glucose-limited chemostat of E. coli LJ110 at D=mu=0.072/h, 28 C, pH 6.6, aerobic (pO2>40%), glucose feed 8 g/L, biomass X~4 g/L. Doubling time td=ln2/mu=0.693/0.072=9.63 h. Glucose uptake derived from chemostat carbon balance assuming residual glucose ~0 (carbon-limited): qGlc = D*(Sf-S)/X = 0.072*(8-0)/4 = 0.144 g/gDW/h; /180.16 g/mol *1000 = 0.80 mmol/gDW/h. Biomass yield Yxs ~4 gDW/L / 8 g/L ~0.5 gDW/g (derived, assuming full glucose consumption). RQ at steady state ~1.1 mol/mol (fully respiratory, no overflow) so acetate secretion negligible at steady state -> secretion_rates empty. The glucose-pulse transient that follows is a perturbation, not a distinct steady growth condition, so not emitted as a separate record. Medium described only as 'defined medium essentially as described previously [ref 12]'; full composition not given in this paper (medium_composition_stated=false).

up.method: glucose biosensor (SenSep, Prussian-blue enzyme chip)

growth_records.cite: Jiang, Zhiming et al. Cofactor Metabolic Engineering of Escherichia coli for Aerobic L-Malate Production with Lower CO2 Emissions. Bioengineering (Basel) (2023). DOI:10.3390/bioengineering10080881. [PMC10451681]
growth_records.snippet: 14.4 g/L L-malate was accumulated from 63.0 g/L glucose with 4.12 g/L succinate and 1.0 g/L pyruvate in E23 ... L-malate yield increased from 0.23 g/g to 0.31 g/g ... E23 ... took 22 h ... highest OD600 ... 11.3 ... malate productivity for
growth_records.notes: Fed-batch, 5-L fermenter. Yield check: 14.4/63.0 = 0.229 g/g (matches reported 0.23). Max OD600 11.3 at 22 h; 1 OD600 = 0.40 g/L DCW so peak biomass ~4.5 g/L DCW. Growth rate not computable (only endpoint OD/time given, fed-batch dilution). Glucose value is total consumed over run, not a specific rate; biomass-specific qGlc not derivable (fed-batch, changing V and X). Reported malate productivity 0.228 g/L/h (20-70 h).

growth_records.notes: Yield check: 16.1/52.5 = 0.307 g/g (matches 0.31). Max OD600 9.4 at 72 h => ~3.76 g/L DCW (1 OD600=0.40 gDCW). nuoA knockout slowed growth. Glucose = total consumed, not specific rate. Reported malate productivity 0.226 g/L/h (72-124 h).

growth_records.snippet: L-malate production by E23 (ΔnuoA, NOG) achieved 21.3 g/L with the yield of 0.43 g/g, which increased by 38.7% compared to 0.31 g/g yield of E23 (ΔnuoA) at the end of 138 h
growth_records.notes: Glucose consumed derived: 21.3 g/L malate / 0.43 g/g = 49.5 g/L glucose (not directly stated; flagged is_experimental:false). Intracellular ATP dropped 18.7% and AcCoA rose 17.2% vs E23(ΔnuoA) (Table 2: ATP 1370±320 vs 1685±416 nmol/gDCW; AcCoA 14.30±0.94 vs 12.20±0.76 nmol/gDCW). Reduced CO2 emission rate in second phase (Fig 7).

growth_records.notes: E. coli BL21(DE3) is a prokaryote. This is a resting/whole-cell bioconversion in 0.1 M potassium phosphate buffer pH 7.5 with 10 mM lactose (NOT a growth-physiology culture); volumetric productivity only (µmol/L/h), no gDW normalization, no growth rate. Baseline E. coli-far fatty alcohol productivity = 58.1 µmol/L/h. Product mainly C16:1 fatty alcohol. Theoretical max yield stated as 0.33 g C16:1 fatty alcohol per g lactose (galactose unused due to gal mutation).

growth_records.snippet: a productivity rate of 165.3 µmol/L/h for E. coli-far-xr ... The total fatty alcohol titer by this engineered cell using lactose was 0.77 mg/ml or 0.22 g/g lactose equivalent in a batch process ... around 22% of the theoretical yield value
growth_records.notes: E. coli BL21(DE3) prokaryote; resting/whole-cell bioconversion in 0.1 M K-phosphate buffer pH 7.5, 10 mM lactose (not a growth culture); volumetric productivity only, no gDW normalization, no growth rate. XR/lactose booster raised fatty alcohol productivity to 165.3 µmol/L/h vs 58.1 µmol/L/h control (3-fold in rich media; 3.8-fold in modified M9). Final titer 0.77 mg/mL (0.77 g/L) = 0.22 g/g lactose = ~22% of 0.33 g/g theoretical C16:1 yield. Bioluminescence (LuxCDEAB) and alkane (FAP) systems in same paper reported only fold-changes (~4x light, 2x alkane, 3x FAD), no absolute rates -> not separate records.

growth_records.cite: Faure et al. A neural-mechanistic hybrid approach improving the predictive power of genome-scale metabolic models. Nat Commun (2023). DOI:10.1038/s41467-023-40380-0. [PMC10400647]
growth_records.snippet: we applied a threshold of 0.165 h-1, which is equal to 5% of the maximum growth rate (3.3 h-1) found in the dataset ... we grew E. coli DH5-alpha in 110 different media compositions, with M9 supplemented ... we experimentally determined the
growth_records.notes: Experimental study: authors grew E. coli DH5-alpha in 110 M9 media (variable carbon sources + 4 amino acids), measuring max specific growth rate per condition by sliding 1 h window linear regression on log(OD600). Only ONE explicit numeric growth-rate value appears in the article text: the maximum specific growth rate across the 110-condition dataset = 3.3 h-1 (stated as basis for the 0.165 h-1 = 5% classification threshold). The 110 individual per-media growth rates are in Fig 3 and the authors' GitHub package, NOT in the article text, so they cannot be transcribed here. Doubling time computed from the reported max: td = ln2/mu = 0.6931/3.3 = 0.210 h (~12.6 min). NOTE: 3.3 h-1 is biologically very high for E. coli (implied doubling ~12.6 min); it is reported verbatim as their OD600 sliding-window regression maximum and is not attributed to any single identified carbon combination, hence medium confidence and the variable carbon-source entry. No substrate uptake or product secretion rates were MEASURED in this paper: FBA uptake-flux upper bounds (e.g., 10, 11, 2.2 mmol gDW-1 h-1) were set arbitrarily/randomly for simulation, not measured. pH not stated for M9 (only the trace-element stock was pH 4). A P. putida KT2440 dataset is also used but is a binary growth/no-growth classification assay with no numeric growth rates, so no record emitted for it.

growth_records.strain: s2208 (s2060 [Addgene #105064] carrying pJC175e [Addgene #79219]) with accessory plasmid + mutagenesis plasmid MP/DP
growth_records.cite: Chuang, Jie-Ning et al. Dissecting the disconnect between circuit activation and dominant adaptive evolution in cytoplasmic phage-assisted continuous evolution (PACE) of an EGFR nanobody. Frontiers in Bioengineering and Biotechnology (2026). DOI:10.3389/fbioe.2026.1829373. [PMC13260496]
growth_records.snippet: This culture was transferred to the chemostat, where the flow rate of fresh media was maintained at a dilution rate of 1 V/h. Once the OD600 of the chemostat remained at 0.4-0.6, AP cells were dispensed to the lagoon at 1 V/h of dilution ra
growth_records.notes: E. coli s2208 is the host chassis for a phage-assisted continuous evolution (PACE) study, not a physiology/13C-MFA subject; the paper reports NO substrate uptake, product secretion, or MFA fluxes. Only one growth parameter is derivable: reported chemostat dilution rate D = 1 V/h (1 vessel volume per hour = 1 /h) with chemostat OD600 held constant at 0.4-0.6 (steady state). At chemostat steady state mu = D, so growth_rate_per_h = 1.0 /h (CALCULATED, not directly measured). doubling_time_h = ln2/mu = 0.6931/1.0 = 0.693 h (CALCULATED). Medium 2xYT is an undefined-composition rich broth (no numeric composition given), so medium_composition_stated=false. Temperature 37 C is the stated host-growth temperature ('s2208 cells were grown in 2xYT ... at 37 C'; transformations/plaque assays also 37 C); chemostat vessel temperature not separately restated. pH not stated for the chemostat. Aerobic assumed (aerobic E. coli culture) though aeration not quantified. Lagoon dilution rates (1, 0.5, 0.25 V/h) describe phage/lagoon flow, not host-cell growth, and are not treated as growth rates. Other three batch papers gave NO prokaryote record: PMC3308132 (Aspergillus niger, a fungus/eukaryote), PMC3909577 (Marchantia linearis, a plant liverwort cell suspension), PMC8198738 (review of microbial molybdenum-to-Mo-blue reduction reporting Mo-blue production/enzyme kinetics e.g. Vmax 7.77 and 0.89 mmole Mo-blue/h, Km, Ki, and qm as a modified mu_m, but no single named-organism experimental condition with medium + measured biomass growth rate + substrate uptake/product secretion).

growth_records.snippet: For planktonic cells, under steady-state conditions, the dilution rate is equal to the specific growth rate of bacteria. A constant cellular concentration was reached for the non-induced planktonic culture, and therefore the specific E. col
growth_records.notes: Discussion section. Non-induced planktonic (suspended) cells in the flow-cell chemostat; at steady state authors state mu = dilution rate and report specific growth rate 0.015 h-1 (experimental, from constant cell density). Feed 0.025 L/h into 1 L recirculating tank gives nominal D=F/V=0.025/1=0.025 h-1 (dilution_rate_per_h); the realised steady-state mu (0.015 h-1) is lower. doubling_time td=ln2/mu=0.693/0.015=46.2 h. Temperature 30 C, LB-Miller + 20 ug/mL kanamycin (complex medium, composition not given -> medium_composition_stated false). No substrate uptake or product secretion rates measured. pH not stated. Induced planktonic culture grew even slower (mu < 0.015 h-1, not quantified) and is not a separate record.

growth_records.notes: Growth rate not stated explicitly; at chemostat steady state mu = D, so mu = 0.6 /h. Doubling time td = ln2/mu = 0.693/0.6 = 1.155 h. Medium: 0.4% (w/v) glucose = 4 g/L (=~22.2 mM, MW 180.16); full MOPS salt composition is only given by citation (Neidhardt et al., ref 48) so only the two explicitly stated components (glucose, 19 mM NH4Cl) are listed. growth_records.snippet: Briefly, using Davis Minimal Medium, E. coli JA122 was evolved in triplicate in aerobic, glucose-limited (0.0125% w/v) chemostats at a fixed dilution rate of 0.2 h-1 and at a constant temperature of 30 degC.
growth_records.notes: Genomics/experimental-evolution paper; the only experimental prokaryote growth condition is the chemostat operating setpoint. At chemostat steady state growth_rate mu = dilution_rate D = 0.2 /h (reported D=0.2 h^-1). doubling_time = ln2/mu = 0.6931/0.2 = 3.47 h (calculated). Glucose 0.0125% w/v = 0.125 g/L (0.0125 g/100 mL); reported as concentration, not as a specific uptake rate. Full Davis Minimal Medium salt composition not itemized in this paper (referenced to prior work ref 25), so medium_composition_stated=false. No measured specific substrate-uptake, product-secretion, MFA fluxes, or biomass yields are reported in this article (it is a whole-genome whole-population sequencing / adaptive-genetics study). NOTE: other dilution rates appearing in the text (mu=0.12 h^-1 and mu=0.6 h^-1) are CITED from another study (ref 126) comparing flagellar expression at slow vs fast dilution, NOT experimental conditions of this paper, so they are excluded.

growth_records.notes: Ancestral E. coli B REL606 baseline for the Lenski LTEE. This paper experimentally MEASURED glucose uptake (glucose oxidase assay), acetate excretion (ion chromatography), growth rate (log fit to OD600), and cell dry weight, plus central-carbon 13C flux ratios (FiatFlux). However ALL absolute numeric values live in supplementary Table S1 (growth params) and Table S2 (flux ratios), which are external PDFs not contained in the fulltext XML, so numeric rate/mu fields are null here. In-text stated constraints: FBA maintenance energy 8.9 mmol ATP/g/h (iAF1260 default); ancestral O2 uptake 11.5-14.75 mmol/gCDW/h taken from Schuetz et al 2007 (cited, not measured). Growth substrate 139 uM glucose (25 mg/L) during selection, raised 10-fold to 1.39 mM for physiological rate assays. No mu/qS arithmetic possible from text alone (ancestral absolutes not given).

growth_records.snippet: strain TH 4-4, which overexpresses GDH-4, achieved the highest L-theanine titer of 9.94 +/- 0.18 g/L, a 68.8% increase relative to the control strain TH 3-3, with a yield of 0.33 g/g.
growth_records.notes: Final engineered strain under standard aerobic shake-flask conditions. Titer 9.94 +/- 0.18 g/L, yield 0.33 g/g xylose. Xylose consumed DERIVED = 9.94/0.33 = 30.1 g/L. Endpoint measurements. This is the same strain later run two-stage and in fed-batch (separate records).

growth_records.snippet: This two-stage strategy effectively enhanced L-theanine production, achieving a titer of 14.31 +/- 0.21 g/L with a yield of 0.48 g/g xylose, which is 1.44 times higher than that under aerobic cultivation conditions.
growth_records.notes: Two-stage microaerobic-aerobic DO strategy (plus sucAB attenuation). Titer 14.31 +/- 0.21 g/L, yield 0.48 g/g xylose (1.44x over single-stage aerobic). Xylose consumed DERIVED = 14.31/0.48 = 29.8 g/L. Oxygen classified microaerophilic due to microaerobic growth phase; production phase aerobic. Endpoint measurements.

up.method: derived from yield: 95.42 g/L theanine / 0.55 g/g = 173.5 g/L; volumetric avg = 173.5/72 = 2.41 g/L/h

growth_records.snippet: L-theanine was accumulated rapidly after 4 h, reaching a peak concentration of 95.42 +/- 2.65 g/L at 72 h. The corresponding yield and productivity were 0.55 g/g xylose and 1.33 g/L/h, respectively. ... cell growth entered the stationary ph
growth_records.notes: Fed-batch 5 L bioreactor, strain TH4-4. Peak L-theanine 95.42 +/- 2.65 g/L at 72 h; yield 0.55 g/g xylose; productivity 1.33 g/L/h (reported; consistent with 95.42/72=1.325). Xylose consumed DERIVED = titer/yield = 95.42/0.55 = 173.5 g/L; avg volumetric xylose uptake = 173.5/72 = 2.41 g/L/h. Note initial medium also contains 10 g/L glucose + 20 g/L xylose; yield reported per g xylose. Biomass reached OD600 ~50 (stationary within 20 h) but no gDW conversion or time-resolved mu given, so specific rates in mmol/gDW/h not computable. pH controlled by 25% ammonium hydroxide (value not stated).

growth_records.snippet: Our highest producer (delta-maeB delta-ndk delta-pykA) reaches a titer of 6.7 mg/L, corresponding to a 7.5-fold increase in C12 fatty acid production.
growth_records.notes: Model-guided (iML1515/OptKnock) engineering paper; the only measured prokaryote physiology output is a C12 fatty-acid TITER of 6.7 mg/L at 24 h (a static amount, not a per-gDW-per-h secretion rate; no biomass/gDW or substrate-uptake measured). Implied BTE-parent baseline = 6.7 / 7.5 = 0.89 mg/L (derived). Growth curves recorded on Bioscreen C (OD600) but no numerical growth rate reported. In-silico exchange bounds in Tables S1/S2 are model constraints, NOT experimental rates, so excluded. Record kept at low confidence to flag the titer only.

growth_records.notes: Authors' own wet 13C-MFA experiment (tracer 0:8:2). Reported directly: mu=0.73+/-0.00 h-1; qGlc=8.91+/-0.53 mmol/gDW/h; qAcetate=4.05+/-0.41 mmol/gDW/h. Doubling time td=ln2/mu=0.6931/0.73=0.949 h. Acetate/glucose molar yield=4.05/8.91=0.455 mol/mol (derived from the two reported rates). No ethanol/other organic acids observed. Preculture 37 C, 120 rpm, M9+3 g/L glucose. This paper also re-uses five LITERATURE flux distributions (Okahashi 2014 continuous culture; Crown 2015 batch MG1655; Toya 2010 pgi-delta and pyk-delta batch; Toya 2007 BW25113 batch) but those absolute rates are not restated in-text (only normalized to glucose=100), so no separate records created for them.

growth_records.snippet: Chemostat conditions were applied at D = 0.3 h-1 for E. coli... with each condition maintained for at least five residence times. ...working volume of 1 L, an agitation speed of 1200 rpm for E. coli... and an aeration rate of 1 VVM. Tempera
growth_records.notes: E. coli glucose-limited chemostat. At steady state specific growth rate equals dilution rate: mu = D = 0.3 h-1 (calculated). Doubling time td = ln2/mu = 0.693/0.3 = 2.31 h. Aerobic (1 VVM), 37C, pH 7, 1200 rpm. Under Segregostat feedback control (1 g glucose/pulse triggered when >50% of population exceeded fluorescence threshold), the effective dilution rate transiently rose to ~0.33 h-1. No experimental substrate uptake or product secretion rates are reported in the text (study focuses on single-cell stress-response entropy via online flow cytometry; a cybernetic MONKS model computes fluxes but numeric qS/qP not given). Co-cultured organism in same paper, S. cerevisiae CEN.PK 113-7D (D=0.1 h-1), is a eukaryote and excluded.

sec.method: Inferred from slope of metabolite concentration time-profiles in Fig. 4 of Li et al. (2016); cited value used as FBA constraint

growth_records.cite: Liebal, Ulf W. et al. CO2 to succinic acid – Estimating the potential of biocatalytic routes. Metabolic Engineering Communications (2018). DOI:10.1016/j.mec.2018.e00075. [PMC6127376]
growth_records.snippet: The experimental acetate uptake and succinate production rate were inferred from the slope of the time profiles of metabolite concentrations of an engineered E. coli mutant presented in Fig. 4 of Li et al. (2016) to 1.6 mmol/gCDW/h (acetate
growth_records.notes: CITED/SECONDARY rate: values are not measured in this (computational FBA) paper; they were inferred by the authors from Fig. 4 time-profiles of an engineered succinate-producing E. coli mutant reported in Li et al. (2016) [ref bib38, cited as 'Li et al., 2016']. Non-growing cells (mu=0), aerobic, acetate as sole C-source. Product yield computed from the two cited rates: Ysucc/acetate = 0.64 mmol/gCDW/h succinate / 1.6 mmol/gCDW/h acetate = 0.40 mol succinate per mol acetate. These rates were used to derive a non-growth NGAM of 1.36 mmol/gCDW/h (Table 2) via FBA on iJO1366. Temperature, pH, and medium composition are not given in this paper (see original Li et al. 2016). No genuinely new wet-lab prokaryote measurement exists in this modeling paper; all other tabulated rates (Synechocystis, C. ljungdahlii, P. pastoris, S. cerevisiae) are either FBA-simulated or borrowed constraints (e.g., C. ljungdahlii substrate/growth from Straub et al. 2014 A. woodii phenotype), hence not emitted as measured records.

growth_records.snippet: After a batch phase... an exponential fed-batch with a specific growth rate of 0.15 (h-1) was initiated.

growth_records.method: reported (max growth rate 0.22 h-1, abstract); td=ln2/0.22 calculated
growth_records.snippet: A maximum growth rate of 0.22 h-1 was obtained... final SA yield of 0.94 g g-1... maximum maintenance production rate of SA under batch conditions was found to be 0.6 g g-1 h-1. Growth terminated ~18 g/L SA, ~3.5 g/L AcA.
growth_records.notes: Batch, 90 g/L glucose (Table 3). Reported mu_max=0.22 h-1 (abstract); td=ln2/0.22=3.15 h. Growth terminates at ~18 g/L SA / ~3.5 g/L AcA. Non-growth maintenance SA production 0.6 g SA/gDW/h reported -> 0.6/118.09*1000=5.08 mmol/gDW/h (twice the chemostat maintenance 0.3 g/gDW/h = 2.54 mmol/gDW/h, from Fig 6 y-intercept). Final (214 h): glucose residual 9.8 g/L (from 90), SA 61.0 g/L, AcA 8.7 g/L, YSP up to 0.94 g/g, SA:AcA incremental up to 5. Specific qGlc not cleanly derivable (time-varying DCW).

up.method: central metabolic fluxes measured by 13C incorporation into protein-bound amino acids on [U-13C]glucose (65 flux samples), but numeric flux/uptake values are only in Supplementary Table S4, not in article text

mfa.note: 13C-MFA performed on a subset of glucose conditions (exponential phase only, triplicate); 65 flux samples. Individual net fluxes are reported only in Supplementary Table S4 (not embedded in the full-text XML), so no numeric values extractable here.

growth_records.cite: Caglar, Mehmet U. et al. The E. coli molecular phenotype under different growth conditions. Scientific Reports (2017). DOI:10.1038/srep45303. [PMC5394689]
growth_records.snippet: we measured doubling times in exponential phase for all experimental conditions (Supplementary Table S5). We found that doubling times varied between 50 and 100 minutes among the various conditions (Fig. 2). Growth was the fastest when gluc
growth_records.notes: E. coli B REL606, Davis Minimal + glucose (DM500). Text states doubling times ranged 50-100 min and glucose was the FASTEST carbon source; the exact per-condition value is in Supplementary Table S5 (not in XML). Attributing the ~50 min lower bound to glucose (explicitly fastest): doubling = 50/60 = 0.833 h, mu = ln2/0.833 = 0.83 h-1. This is an inference from the stated range extreme, hence confidence medium. 13C-MFA (65 flux samples) and central-metabolic flux via [U-13C]glucose were done but numeric fluxes/uptakes reside in Supplementary Table S4 only, so left null (is_experimental true, values not in text).

growth_records.cite: Caglar, Mehmet U. et al. The E. coli molecular phenotype under different growth conditions. Scientific Reports (2017). DOI:10.1038/srep45303. [PMC5394689]
growth_records.snippet: doubling times varied between 50 and 100 minutes among the various conditions (Fig. 2). Growth was the fastest when glucose was used as carbon source and the slowest when the carbon source was lactose.
growth_records.notes: Lactose was explicitly the SLOWEST carbon source; attributing the ~100 min upper bound of the stated 50-100 min range to lactose: doubling = 100/60 = 1.667 h, mu = ln2/1.667 = 0.42 h-1. Inference from range extreme (per-condition value only in Supplementary Table S5), hence confidence medium. Other carbon sources tested (glycerol, gluconate, lactate at 0.5 g/L) fall between these extremes but have no individually stated doubling time in the text, so no records emitted for them to avoid invention.

up.method: derived qS=(Sin)*D/X assuming complete consumption of 21.9 mM feed glucose (carbon-limited)

sec.method: reported specific acetate production rate for the anaerobic steady state
derived qP=C*D/X from measured 35.1 mM (NMR) at anaerobic steady state

growth_records.cite: Trotter EW et al. Reprogramming of Escherichia coli K-12 Metabolism during the Initial Phase of Transition from an Anaerobic to a Micro-Aerobic Environment. PLoS ONE (2011). DOI:10.1371/journal.pone.0025501. [PMC3181329]
growth_records.snippet: the specific rate of acetate production for the anaerobic steady-state was ~8.2 mmoles h-1 g-1 cdw ... Time 0: Acetate 18.7 mM, Formate 35.1 mM, Succinate 5.3 mM, Pyruvate <0.10 mM, Lactate 0.12 mM, Ethanol 9.7 mM, Biomass 0.46 mg cdw ml-1
growth_records.notes: Anaerobic glucose-limited chemostat steady state, D=0.2 h-1 so mu=D=0.2 h-1; td=ln2/0.2=3.47 h. Biomass X=0.46 mg cdw/ml=0.46 g/L (Table 3, 44.3% C, carbon balance 96%). Steady-state secretion rate qP=C*D/X. Acetate: 18.7*0.2/0.46=8.13 (paper reports ~8.2, confirms). Formate: 35.1*0.2/0.46=15.26. Ethanol: 9.7*0.2/0.46=4.22. Succinate: 5.3*0.2/0.46=2.30. Lactate: 0.12*0.2/0.46=0.052. Glucose uptake qS=21.9*0.2/0.46=9.52 assuming complete consumption of feed (carbon-limited). Mixed-acid fermentation product profile. Only acetate rate explicitly stated in paper; others derived from measured NMR concentrations at steady state.

sec.method: reported specific acetate production rate for the 60-120 min micro-aerobic transition period
transient excretion; measured extracellular pyruvate peaked at 0.99 mM at 60 min (0.90 at 30, 0.41 at 10, <0.10 at 0), then net consumption by 120 min (0.23 mM)

growth_records.cite: Trotter EW et al. Reprogramming of Escherichia coli K-12 Metabolism during the Initial Phase of Transition from an Anaerobic to a Micro-Aerobic Environment. PLoS ONE (2011). DOI:10.1371/journal.pone.0025501. [PMC3181329]
growth_records.snippet: ~6.2 mmoles h-1 g-1 cdw for the 60-120 min period of the transition i.e. after transition acetate production was sub-maximal but non-zero, indicative of micro-aerobic growth ... transient excretion of pyruvate
growth_records.notes: Same chemostat (D=0.2 h-1, mu=0.2), but this is a dynamic (non-steady-state) transition from anaerobic to micro-aerobic (10 uM O2), so rates are transient. Acetate ~6.2 mmol/gDW/h explicitly reported for 60-120 min window (vs 8.2 anaerobic). Pyruvate transiently excreted (Table 3, mM): 0(t0),0.41(10),0.90(30),0.99(60),0.23(120) - peaks at 60 min then net consumed. Succinate/formate/ethanol concentrations decline over transition (Table 3). Biomass rises 0.46->0.65 mg cdw/ml over 120 min. Reported qAcetateMax anaerobic=0% aerobiosis reference.

up.compound: glucose (measured with fluorescent analogue 2-NBDG, 30 uM in M9)
up.units: arbitrary fluorescence units (a.u.), single-cell whole-cell intensity
up.method: Single-cell time-lapse fluorescence of 2-NBDG uptake over 15 min (n=40 old/new pole daughter pairs); new pole daughters accumulate significantly more glucose than old pole daughters (paired t-test). Not convertible to mmol/gDW/h.

growth_records.cite: Lapinska U et al. Bacterial ageing in the absence of external stressors. Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society B: Biological Sciences (2019). DOI:10.1098/rstb.2018.0442. [PMC6792439]
growth_records.snippet: the parental cell divides asymmetrically, with the old pole daughter showing a longer doubling time and slower glucose accumulation than the new pole daughter ... doubling time ... progressively increases with successive rounds of cell divi
growth_records.notes: Single-cell ageing study of wild-type E. coli BW25113 in a mother machine under continuous LB perfusion at 37C. Growth physiology = per-cell doubling times measured over generations, but NO absolute doubling-time or growth-rate value is given in the text (data only in Fig 2 as generation-dependent curves): old pole daughters divide slower than new pole daughters (LMM chi2_1=5.192, p=0.023) and doubling time rises then asymptotes over 24 generations. Glucose uptake measured only as relative single-cell 2-NBDG fluorescence (a.u.), not as a rate; new pole > old pole. No numeric mu/qS/qP extractable, so all rate fields left null. LB composition: 10 g/L tryptone, 5 g/L yeast extract, 0.5 g/L NaCl. Additional strains (BW25113 tnaC-GFP, BL21 expressing Abeta1-42) were used only as positive controls for aggregate formation and reported no growth rates.

growth_records.cite: Fernández-Sandoval, Marco T et al. Xylose–glucose co-fermentation to ethanol by Escherichia coli strain MS04 using single- and two-stage continuous cultures under micro-aerated conditions. Microbial Cell Factories (2019). DOI:10.1186/s12934-019-1191-0. [PMC6706898]
growth_records.snippet: In batch culture, the total concentration of sugars (50 g/L) was consumed in 18 h, with an ethanol production of 22 g/L, and a cell mass concentration close to 3 g_DCW/L ... the cell yield on consumed sugars (Y_XS) was only 0.054 g/g
growth_records.notes: Table 1 batch row: mu=0.46 h-1 (reported), q_P=1.38 g_EtOH/gDCW/h, Q_P=1.3 g/L/h, Y_PS=87% of 0.51 g/g theoretical, Y_XS=0.054 g/g, carbon balance 91%. Doubling time td=ln2/mu=0.693/0.46=1.507 h. Derived qS = mu/Y_XS = 0.46/0.054 = 8.52 g sugar/gDCW/h (total mixed sugars, cannot split per-sugar). Ethanol qP in molar = 1.38/46.07*1000 = 29.95 mmol/gDCW/h. Acetate: 25% of 2 g/L consumed = 0.5 g/L. AM1 full salt composition not stated (cited ref 36); only carbon/supplements given. Cell conversion 1 OD600 = 0.37 gDCW/L.

growth_records.medium: SMM (Standard Mineral Medium, ref [18]) + 10 mM glycerol

up.method: calculated average specific rate: delta-glycerol / (avg biomass x time)
measured volumetric consumption: (10.12-0.07) mM / 10 h

growth_records.cite: Martin Demko et al. Computational Modelling of Metabolic Burden and Substrate Toxicity in Escherichia coli Carrying a Synthetic Metabolic Pathway. Microorganisms (2019). DOI:10.3390/microorganisms7110553. [PMC6921056]
growth_records.snippet: the particular results for CDW starting at 0.0429 and ending at 0.468 g/L after 10 h of growth; and (right) the result for the substrate utilisation only starting at 10.12 and ending at 0.07 mM after 10 h ... The experimental data are pictu
growth_records.notes: E. coli deg31 growth on SMM mineral medium with 10 mM glycerol as sole carbon source; glycerol is the harmless final product of the engineered TCP-degradation pathway and here serves as the growth substrate. Full SMM composition not given in-text (cited ref [18]); only 10 mM glycerol carbon source stated -> medium_composition_stated=false. pH not reported. MEASURED endpoints from Figure 6 model-fit-to-experimental-data (points are experimental): CDW 0.0429->0.468 g/L over 10 h; glycerol 10.12->0.07 mM over 10 h. Measured doubling-time range 2.58-9.29 h (median of >=3 experiments spanning IPTG induction 0-1 mM; metabolic-burden slows growth at higher IPTG). DERIVED: (1) growth_rate 0.239/h = ln(0.468/0.0429)/10h = ln(10.909)/10 = 2.3897/10 (apparent exponential mu from CDW endpoints); reported doubling-time range gives mu = ln2/td = 0.693/9.29 = 0.075/h (slow) to 0.693/2.58 = 0.269/h (fast); listed doubling_time_h=2.9 corresponds to the mu=0.239 endpoint estimate (0.693/0.239). (2) Glycerol volumetric uptake = (10.12-0.07 mM)/10 h = 1.005 mmol/L/h. (3) Specific glycerol uptake qS = 1.005 / avg_biomass, avg_biomass=(0.0429+0.468)/2=0.2555 g/L -> qS = 3.93 mmol/gDW/h (average over run). (4) Yxs = biomass produced / glycerol consumed = (0.468-0.0429 g/L)/(10.05e-3 mol/L x 92.09 g/mol) = 0.4251/0.9255 = 0.459 gDW/g glycerol (glycerol MW=92.09). Paper is primarily a modeling study but these growth/substrate values are its own real experimental data used for model calibration; no product secretion measured.

Reported specific rates (per gCDW) from Table 1. Unit conversions to mmol/gDW/h: glucose 0.63/180.16*1000=3.50; NH4+ 0.04/18.04*1000=2.22; acetate 0.10/60.05*1000=1.67. O2 and CO2 already in mmol/gDW/h. Yields Yxs=0.32, Yxn=4.63. Excess residual glucose in STR 2.07±0.25 g/L, acetate 1.29-1.39 g/L. Carbon recovery 101±2%. Temperature 37°C (continuous chemostat phase), pH 7.0 (controlled with 3M NaOH/2.5M H3PO4). No 13C-MFA in this paper.

growth_records.snippet: The observed cell growth was 0.47 gDCW/L at 24 h of culture. HA01 Glucose consumption rate 0.1195 g/L/h, Galactose 0.0015 g/L/h, Cell mass 0.47 gDCW/L, Specific growth rate 0.0366 /h, HA titer N.D.
growth_records.notes: Values verbatim from Table 2 (batch culture results). Specific growth rate 0.0366/h reported; doubling_time = ln2/mu = 0.6931/0.0366 = 18.94 h. Consumption rates are VOLUMETRIC (g/L/h) as reported. Conversion to molar: glucose MW 180.16 g/mol -> 0.1195/180.16*1000 = 0.663 mmol/L/h. Approx specific glucose uptake qS ~ R_Glc/max_biomass = 0.1195/0.47 = 0.254 g/gDCW/h = 1.41 mmol/gDCW/h (APPROXIMATE: uses max DCW at 24h, biomass changes over batch). HA01 host lacks galactose utilization under dual-sugar condition; no HA produced.

growth_records.cite: Satanowski et al. Awakening a latent carbon fixation cycle in Escherichia coli. Nature Communications (2020). DOI:10.1038/s41467-020-19564-5. [PMC7669889]
growth_records.snippet: The observed growth rate via the GED shunt is almost half of that obtained with gluconate, which requires no carboxylation by Gnd and thus serves as a positive control (doubling times 6.9 and 2.8 h, respectively).
growth_records.notes: mu calculated from reported doubling time: mu = ln2/td = 0.693/6.9 = 0.1004 /h. No uptake/secretion or MFA net-flux values reported (only 13C amino-acid labeling patterns, qualitative). Growth strictly dependent on 20% CO2 and on overexpression of gnd/edd/eda (pGED); gnd-only (pG) or edd/eda-only (pED) gave <2 doublings.

growth_records.snippet: In substrate-limited (i.e., C-limited) fed-batch conditions with specific substrate uptake rates distinctly lower than qS,max (here: qS < 1.1 g/gh), the strain shows no acetate formation and the biomass yield increases to YX/S = 0.5 g/g.
growth_records.notes: Fed-batch qS setpoint 0.6 g glucose/(gDW.h) -> molar 0.6/180.16 = 3.33 mmol/gDW/h. Acetate-free threshold qS<1.1 g/gh -> 1.1/180.16 = 6.11 mmol/gDW/h. mu at steady state derived as mu = qS*Yx/s = 0.6 g/gh * 0.5 g/g = 0.30 /h; td = ln2/mu = 0.693/0.30 = 2.31 h. Yxs improves to 0.50 g/g under C-limitation (vs 0.45 in excess batch) and no acetate is excreted. Base medium per DeLisa et al., not fully itemized -> medium_composition_stated=false.

growth_records.notes: 13C-MFA/thermodynamics paper covering E. coli (prokaryote), yeast and mammalian iBMK; only E. coli recorded here. growth_records.cite: Christopher J. Skalnik et al. Whole-cell modeling of E. coli colonies enables quantification of single-cell heterogeneity in antibiotic responses. PLOS Computational Biology (2023). DOI:10.1371/journal.pcbi.1011232. [PMC10310025]
growth_records.snippet: we noted the measured doubling time of E. coli strain B/r (44 minutes), whose physiological and growth measurements were used to parameterize the model
growth_records.notes: This is a computational whole-cell/whole-colony modeling paper; its own reported doubling time (50.89 +/- 4.61 min) and per-cell glucose uptake distributions are SIMULATION outputs, not experimental measurements, so they are excluded. The single EXPERIMENTAL prokaryote datum is the cited measured doubling time of E. coli B/r = 44 min, taken from the model's parameterization reference (not measured in this study). Converted: doubling_time = 44 min = 44/60 = 0.7333 h; mu = ln2/td = 0.693/0.7333 = 0.945 /h. No experimental in-study uptake or secretion rates and no experimental medium composition for the B/r datum are reported here (medium_composition_stated:false; the 1 mM glucose M9 refers to the simulation environment). Temperature 37C assumed for E. coli B/r balanced growth (standard, not explicitly stated for the cited value).

growth_records.notes: E. coli central-metabolism kinetic-constraint modelling paper. At steady state in a chemostat mu = dilution rate D, so mu = 0.2 h-1 for the parental BW25113 condition explicitly named. doubling_time = ln2/mu = 0.6931/0.2 = 3.47 h (calculated). Measured glucose uptake and CO2 secretion exist for this condition but all rate DATA are cited FROM Ishii et al. 2007 (Science; ref 16) and are displayed only graphically (Fig 6) — no numeric mmol/gDW/h values appear in this article's text, so rates left null with is_experimental:true and citation noted. Only CO2 secreted (no acetate/ethanol/formate — fermentation blocked).

Only CO2 excreted (fermentation blocked). NOTE: this paper draws on ~25 Ishii conditions (parental at several dilution rates + single-gene knockouts); only D=0.2 h-1 and D=0.5 h-1 for the parental strain are explicitly given in the text, so only those two are emitted; the knockout-mutant conditions are not individually resolvable from this article's text.

growth_records.cite: Hideyuki Yaginuma et al. Diversity in ATP concentrations in a single bacterial cell population revealed by quantitative single-cell imaging. Scientific Reports (2014). DOI:10.1038/srep06522. [PMC4185378]
growth_records.snippet: The chemostat culture volume was maintained within 125-140 ml, and medium feeding was 15 ml/h, giving a dilution rate of 0.10-0.12 h-1. ... after the optical density (OD) reached ~0.2 and the growth rate became limited by the dilution rate
growth_records.notes: ATP-biosensor methods paper; no substrate uptake or product secretion rates measured (metabolic-physiology data limited to growth rate). E. coli grown in glucose-limited chemostat on low-glucose (1 mM) modified M63; steady-state specific growth rate = dilution rate. Text explicitly states growth became limited by the dilution rate 0.10-0.12 h-1, so mu = D = 0.10-0.12 h-1 -> midpoint 0.11 h-1 recorded for both dilution_rate_per_h and growth_rate_per_h. doubling_time = ln2/mu = 0.6931/0.11 = 6.3 h. Base (normal) M63 for batch pre-culture is identical but with 10 mM glucose (pH 7.0, adjusted with KOH); batch runs kept OD600 < 0.6 with no reported growth rate, so no batch record made. Chemostat temperature not explicitly stated in Methods (microscope imaging was at 25 C) -> temperature_C left null, not assumed. Imaging strain identity not named in text; JM109(DE3) was the biosensor purification host, not confirmed as the chemostat/imaging host.

growth_records.snippet: Through ALE, mu increased from 0.7 h-1 for wild-type ... ALE endpoint strains achieved a wide ranging q_ac from 3.9-11.4 mmol gDW-1 h-1 (where wild-type q_ac was 3.9 mmol gDW-1 h-1).
growth_records.notes: E. coli K-12 MG1655 adapted at 37C in liquid minimal medium containing glucose (medium composition not itemized in main text; cited from LaCroix et al. 2015, ref 3). Wild-type specific growth rate mu=0.7 h-1 (reported). Doubling time = ln2/mu = 0.6931/0.7 = 0.990 h. Wild-type acetate secretion q_ac=3.9 mmol/gDW/h (experimental). Glucose uptake rate q_glc was measured for all strains but the numeric wild-type value is in supplementary S8 Table (XLSX) not in the parsed XML text, so rate=null with is_experimental:true. No pH given.

sec.method: Quantitative q_ac; text reports range 3.9-11.4 mmol/gDW/h across the 8 ALE endpoint strains (per-strain numbers in supplementary XLSX)

growth_records.cite: Chuankai Cheng et al. Laboratory evolution reveals a two-dimensional rate-yield tradeoff in microbial metabolism. PLoS Computational Biology (2019). DOI:10.1371/journal.pcbi.1007066. [PMC6564042]
growth_records.snippet: Diversity of metabolic phenotypes was observed in the ALE endpoint strains. Through ALE, mu increased from 0.7 h-1 for wild-type to 0.95-1.1 ... ALE endpoint strains achieved a wide ranging q_ac from 3.9-11.4 mmol gDW-1 h-1.
growth_records.notes: Aggregate record for the 8 ALE endpoint isolates of E. coli K-12 MG1655 (glucose minimal medium, 37C, aerobic batch). Measured quantities per text: specific growth rate mu range 0.95-1.1 h-1; acetate secretion q_ac range 3.9-11.4 mmol/gDW/h; glucose uptake q_glc measured. Per-strain numeric mu/q_glc/q_ac are in supplementary tables S7/S8 (XLSX) not in parsed XML, hence single growth_rate/doubling_time fields left null and ranges captured in notes/snippet. Two of the eight endpoints were additionally characterized by 13C-MFA (uptake+secretion by HPLC). These are primary experimental measurements reported in this study (strains are replicates from LaCroix et al. 2015, PMID 25304508). No pH stated.

growth_records.strain: Histidine-requiring laboratory strain, ancestor R0 (genome mapped to MDS42, AP012306)

up.method: Growth-limiting substrate; Monod substrate affinity K_S ~0.2 uM histidine reported for R0, but no per-biomass consumption rate measured

growth_records.cite: Bei-Wen Ying et al. Evolutionary Consequence of a Trade-Off between Growth and Maintenance along with Ribosomal Damages. PLoS ONE (2015). DOI:10.1371/journal.pone.0135639. [PMC4546238]
growth_records.snippet: Fitting the experimentally evaluated growth rate data (Fig 3B) with the Monod equation ... verified that the maximal growth rate (r_max) decreased from ~0.69 to 0.14 h-1, and the substrate affinity (K_S) was likely improved from ~0.2 to 0.0
growth_records.notes: Ancestor R0 of a histidine-requiring E. coli in M63 minimal medium (full salt+glucose composition stated; 22 mM glucose) with histidine as growth-limiting nutrient, 37C, aerobic, 200 rpm batch. Maximal growth rate r_max ~0.69 h-1 (reported from Monod fit of growth rate vs histidine, Fig 3B/3C). Doubling time = ln2/0.69 = 0.6931/0.69 = 1.00 h. Monod K_S(histidine) ~0.2 uM. Growth rates were reported graphically (Monod fit); only r_max stated numerically. Histidine consumption per-biomass rate not measured, so uptake rate=null. During the evolution experiment cells were grown at 10 uM histidine to saturation (~3x10^8 cells/mL); characterization here used 100 uM histidine.

growth_records.strain: Evolved population R7 (after 7 rounds of starvation/resuscitation; ribosomal rpsI/rpsA + rnr mutations)

up.method: Growth-limiting substrate; Monod K_S improved to ~0.0008 uM histidine for R7 (higher affinity), but no per-biomass consumption rate measured

growth_records.cite: Bei-Wen Ying et al. Evolutionary Consequence of a Trade-Off between Growth and Maintenance along with Ribosomal Damages. PLoS ONE (2015). DOI:10.1371/journal.pone.0135639. [PMC4546238]
growth_records.snippet: the maximal growth rate (r_max) decreased from ~0.69 to 0.14 h-1, and the substrate affinity (K_S) was likely improved from ~0.2 to 0.0008 uM, under the conditions of repeated starvation and resuscitation.
growth_records.notes: Evolved endpoint R7 of the histidine-requiring E. coli in M63 minimal medium + histidine, 37C, aerobic, 200 rpm batch. Maximal growth rate r_max=0.14 h-1 (reported, Monod fit; dramatic loss of growth fitness in rich conditions vs ancestor). Doubling time = ln2/0.14 = 0.6931/0.14 = 4.95 h. Monod K_S(histidine) ~0.0008 uM (improved affinity, enabling growth on trace histidine). Growth reported graphically/Monod; no uptake or secretion per-biomass rates measured. Slow growth attributed to ribosomal-protein (rpsI/rpsA) and rnr frameshift mutations.

growth_records.snippet: The growth rate improved significantly from 0.017 h-1 (MeSV2.1) to 0.081 +/- 0.002 h-1 (MeSV2.2) ... Growth curves of the evolution endpoint ... for strains MeSV2.1 on 5 mM gluconate with 0.1 g L-1 yeast extract
growth_records.notes: mu = 0.017 h-1 reported (exponential regression on OD600, n=3; CDW correlation 0.33 gCDW/L per OD600). Doubling time computed by curator: td = ln2/mu = 0.6931/0.017 = 40.8 h. Grown on 5 mM gluconate + 500 mM methanol + 0.1 g/L yeast extract, shake-flask batch, 37C, 160 rpm, aerobic. Methanol uptake rate NOT measured for this strain (only MeSV2.2). Reference methanol uptakes cited from other papers (15 mmol/gCDW/h natural methylotroph [ref 44]; 0.6 mmol/gCDW/h prior E. coli [ref 28]) - not this strain's data.

growth_records.cite: Meyer, Fabian et al. Methanol-essential growth of Escherichia coli. Nature Communications (2018). DOI:10.1038/s41467-018-03937-y. [PMC5904121]
growth_records.snippet: MeSV2.2 had a methanol consumption rate of 13 +/- 7 mmol gCDW-1 h-1, which is in the same range as previously reported for natural methylotrophs (15 mmol gCDW-1 h-1) ... only a small accumulation of formic acid (0.09 +/- 0.01 mM, in mid exp
growth_records.notes: mu = 0.081 +/- 0.002 h-1 reported (exponential regression, n=3). Curator computed doubling time: td = ln2/mu = 0.6931/0.081 = 8.56 h. Experimental methanol uptake qMeOH = 13 +/- 7 mmol/gCDW/h (PRIORITY experimental rate). Formate 0.09 mM is a supernatant concentration, not a per-biomass rate. 13C-labeling: up to 23.9% methanol carbon into hexose-6-P, 11-24% across central metabolites. CDW factor 0.33 gCDW/L per OD600. Batch shake-flask, 37C, 160 rpm, aerobic. Reference uptake values (15 and 0.6 mmol/gCDW/h) are cited from other papers, not this strain.

growth_records.snippet: For E. coli, during growth with acetate as electron donor, the external medium environment became basic and acid was added to maintain neutral pH ... The maximum observed OD 550 nm value was 0.45 and substrate was completely consumed after
growth_records.notes: E. coli W3110 aerobic on acetate 10 mM: net proton CONSUMPTION, confirms iJR904 prediction. Derived average volumetric acetate uptake = 10 mM / 25 h = 0.4 mmol/L/h (arithmetic: 10/25). This is volumetric, NOT per-biomass; cannot convert to mmol/gDW/h without biomass integral (initial/final biomass in Table S1, unavailable). Final biomass estimate: OD550 0.45 x 0.33 gDW/L per OD = 0.1485 gDW/L. Max OD550 = 0.45 reported.

growth_records.cite: Yanfang Feng et al. Experimental Simulation of the Effects of an Initial Antibiotic Treatment on a Subsequent Treatment after Initial Therapy Failure. Antibiotics (Basel) (2014). DOI:10.3390/antibiotics3010049. [PMC4790345]
growth_records.method: calculated (mu = dilution rate D at chemostat steady state; td = ln2/mu)
growth_records.snippet: Amoxicillin treatment was initiated after all the parameters, including the culture's OD, reached steady state at a dilution rate (D) of 0.3 h-1.
growth_records.notes: Antibiotic-resistance study, not a flux paper; NO substrate uptake or product secretion rates are reported. Growth record only. At chemostat steady state mu = D = 0.3 h-1 (glucose-limited). doubling_time = ln2/0.3 = 0.6931/0.3 = 2.31 h. Medium: batch cultures used Evan's medium with 100 mM Na2HPO4 buffer and 55 mM glucose, pH 6.9, 37C; for continuous cultures glucose and Na2HPO4 were reduced to 5 mM and 10 mM respectively (values listed here are the continuous-culture record). Evan's medium is a defined minimal mineral medium; full salt/trace composition not enumerated in-text (cited as ref 27). Represents the pre-antibiotic (0 ug/mL amoxicillin) steady state.

up.units: experimental glucose uptake rate used for model fitting/validation (from cited literature refs 13,34-37; Ishii et al. 2007 Science); numeric value not stated in this paper text

growth_records.cite: Millard, Pierre et al. Metabolic regulation is sufficient for global and robust coordination of glucose uptake, catabolism, energy production and growth in Escherichia coli. PLoS Computational Biology (2017). DOI:10.1371/journal.pcbi.1005396. [PMC5328398]
growth_records.snippet: steady-state and time-course experimental data obtained from a unique E. coli strain (the model strain K-12 MG1655) grown under a unique reference condition (M9 minimal medium with glucose as sole carbon source, dilution rate = 0.1 h-1, tem
growth_records.notes: Kinetic-model paper. The reference growth condition is an aerobic glucose-limited chemostat: D=0.1 /h, 37C, pH 7.0, pO2>20%, M9 + glucose, E. coli K-12 MG1655. At steady state mu=D=0.1 /h (calculated); doubling_time=ln2/mu=0.693/0.1=6.93 h. Experimental validation data (growth rate, glucose and oxygen uptake rates, citrate-synthase/TCA fluxes, acetate) were taken from literature (refs 13,34-37; Ishii et al. 2007 Science 316:593) and shown as simulated-vs-experimental scatter in Figs 4-6; NO explicit numeric measured exchange-flux values appear in the readable text, so uptake/secretion rates left null (not invented). Model fluxes are internal (mM/s). M9 salt composition not itemised in text -> medium_composition_stated=false.

up.method: batch time-course, linear least-squares (UPLC)

growth_records.notes: Experimental (mu_exp) aerobic specific growth rate = 0.56 +/- 0.03 h-1 (Table 6). Doubling time calculated td=ln2/mu=0.6931/0.56=1.238 h. Measured glucose uptake = 15.5 mmol/gDW/h (Table 6 footnote b, 'from this work', determined for the K-12 lineage representative MG1655; aerobic). Medium = MOPS minimal (TekNova) + glucose 11 mM (also described as 0.2% glucose ~11 mM); full MOPS salt composition not enumerated in paper so medium_composition_stated=false. Growth yield reported in Figure 6/7 but only graphically (no numeric value in text). O2 uptake 15 mmol/gDW/h used in FBA was cited from W3110 ref [73], not measured here.

up.method: batch OD600 growth curve; no numeric uptake rate given

sec.method: Megazyme Acetic Acid Assay Kit on frozen supernatant; acetate secreted during exponential glucose phase then re-consumed in a diauxic second phase; measured only as relative slope (acetate conc vs OD), no absolute mmol/gDW/h value in text

yields.Yxs: overall biomass yield on glucose reported graphically (slope of produced biomass ΔB vs initial nutrient amount, Fig S1, R2>0.9); numeric value not stated in text

growth_records.cite: Golan, Ohad et al. Overall biomass yield on multiple nutrient sources. NPJ Systems Biology and Applications (2025). DOI:10.1038/s41540-025-00497-y. [PMC11811147]
growth_records.snippet: we follow growth of E. coli until depletion of the initial nutrient source ... in M9 minimal medium with glucose, malate, or aspartate as sole carbon sources. These carbon sources were chosen as respiro-fermentative, strictly respiratory, a
growth_records.notes: Experimental prokaryote batch growth, E. coli NCM3722, M9 minimal + glucose, 37C, 350 rpm aerobic. Measured physiology is OVERALL BIOMASS YIELD (Yxs) = slope of produced biomass (ΔB = stationary biomass - inoculum biomass) vs initial glucose amount; reported ONLY graphically (Fig S1), no numeric Yxs in text. OD600 converted to dry weight using cited calibration 0.396 gDW/(L*OD) (from ref 18, Basan et al.-type conversion, NOT measured here). No numeric growth rate, uptake rate, or MFA reported; authors explicitly note the model 'is limited by its exclusion of dynamic data, such as metabolic flux rates and growth rates.' Acetate secretion measured (assay kit) but only as a relative slope, no absolute rate. M9 salt composition not enumerated in text. Carbon amount titrated 0-1.2 g/l; seed/pre-culture used 4 g/l glucose at 37C 350 rpm.

up.method: batch OD600 growth curve; no numeric uptake rate

yields.Yxs: overall biomass yield on malate = slope of produced biomass vs initial malate amount (Fig 1B, linear fit R2>0.9); numeric value not stated in text

growth_records.cite: Golan, Ohad et al. Overall biomass yield on multiple nutrient sources. NPJ Systems Biology and Applications (2025). DOI:10.1038/s41540-025-00497-y. [PMC11811147]
growth_records.snippet: The produced biomass shows a good linear fit to the initial amount of the sole carbon source (Fig. 1B) and as such, can be described by ΔB = Y_X/D * N_D ... The slope of the linear fit is the overall biomass yield (fit parameter R2 > 0.9).
growth_records.notes: E. coli NCM3722, M9 minimal + malate (strictly respiratory C source), aerobic batch, 37C. Overall biomass yield reported graphically only (Fig 1B slope), no numeric Yxs, no growth rate, no uptake/secretion rate, no MFA in text. OD->DW via cited 0.396 gDW/(L*OD). M9 salts not enumerated.

up.method: batch OD600 growth curve; no numeric uptake rate

yields.Yxs: overall biomass yield on aspartate = slope of produced biomass vs initial aspartate amount (Fig S1, linear fit R2>0.9); numeric value not stated in text

growth_records.cite: Golan, Ohad et al. Overall biomass yield on multiple nutrient sources. NPJ Systems Biology and Applications (2025). DOI:10.1038/s41540-025-00497-y. [PMC11811147]
growth_records.snippet: in M9 minimal medium with glucose, malate, or aspartate as sole carbon sources ... a respiro-fermentative, strictly respiratory, and a degradable biomass component. Data for glucose and aspartate experiments are shown in Fig. S1.
growth_records.notes: E. coli NCM3722, M9 minimal + aspartate, aerobic batch, 37C. Overall biomass yield graphical only (Fig S1 slope); no numeric Yxs, growth rate, uptake/secretion rate, or MFA in text. OD->DW via cited 0.396 gDW/(L*OD). M9 salts not enumerated. NOTE: paper also studies co-utilization (xylose or methionine as 'measured' nutrient on glucose/acetate/succinate base nutrients, and aspartate on glucose/acetate base) with diauxic two-phase growth on glucose->secreted acetate; all yields graphical, no numeric rates.

growth_records.cite: Wilson, Jayne Louise et al. CO-Releasing Molecules Have Nonheme Targets in Bacteria: Transcriptomic, Mathematical Modeling and Biochemical Analyses of CORM-3 [Ru(CO)3Cl(glycinate)] Actions on a Heme-Deficient Mutant of Escherichia coli. Antioxidants & Redox Signaling (2015). DOI:10.1089/ars.2014.6151. [PMC4492677]
growth_records.snippet: Wild-type cultures stressed with 100 μM CORM-3 showed a marginally increased doubling time (0.81±0.18 h) compared with the control (0.79±0.13 h)
growth_records.notes: E. coli K-12 MG1655 wild type, UNTREATED control, anaerobic defined medium + glucose, 37 C, pH 7. Reported doubling time td = 0.79 ± 0.13 h. mu = ln2/td = 0.693/0.79 = 0.877 h-1 (calculated). No uptake/secretion rates measured (transcriptomic/growth study). Mixed-acid fermentation expected (heme-independent) but products not quantified.

growth_records.strain: K-12 MG1655 (wild type) + 100 uM CORM-3 stress
growth_records.cite: Wilson, Jayne Louise et al. CO-Releasing Molecules Have Nonheme Targets in Bacteria: Transcriptomic, Mathematical Modeling and Biochemical Analyses of CORM-3 [Ru(CO)3Cl(glycinate)] Actions on a Heme-Deficient Mutant of Escherichia coli. Antioxidants & Redox Signaling (2015). DOI:10.1089/ars.2014.6151. [PMC4492677]
growth_records.snippet: Wild-type cultures stressed with 100 μM CORM-3 showed a marginally increased doubling time (0.81±0.18 h)... at 200 and 300 μM CORM-3, growth was prevented for 5 h.
growth_records.notes: E. coli MG1655 WT + 100 uM CORM-3, anaerobic. Reported td = 0.81 ± 0.18 h. mu = ln2/0.81 = 0.856 h-1 (calculated). Marginal growth reduction vs control (0.79 h). At 200/300 uM growth prevented ~5 h (no rate). No uptake/secretion rates.

growth_records.snippet: Similar results were obtained for the hemA mutant; 100 μM CORM-3 increased the doubling time from 2.0±0.33 h (control) to 3.6±1.3 h.
growth_records.notes: E. coli hemA heme-deficient mutant, UNTREATED control, anaerobic, 37 C, pH 7 (+50 ug/mL kanamycin). Reported td = 2.0 ± 0.33 h. mu = ln2/2.0 = 0.347 h-1 (calculated). Grows slower than WT (heme-deficient, fermentative only). No uptake/secretion rates.

growth_records.cite: Wilson, Jayne Louise et al. CO-Releasing Molecules Have Nonheme Targets in Bacteria: Transcriptomic, Mathematical Modeling and Biochemical Analyses of CORM-3 [Ru(CO)3Cl(glycinate)] Actions on a Heme-Deficient Mutant of Escherichia coli. Antioxidants & Redox Signaling (2015). DOI:10.1089/ars.2014.6151. [PMC4492677]
growth_records.snippet: 100 μM CORM-3 increased the doubling time from 2.0±0.33 h (control) to 3.6±1.3 h. Unlike the control, mutant cultures did not recover even after 28 h of incubation with the CORM
growth_records.notes: E. coli hemA mutant + 100 uM CORM-3, anaerobic. Reported td = 3.6 ± 1.3 h. mu = ln2/3.6 = 0.193 h-1 (calculated). Heme-deficient mutant markedly more CORM-sensitive than WT (WT 0.79->0.81 h; mutant 2.0->3.6 h) - the paper's central finding that CORMs have nonheme targets. No uptake/secretion rates. L. lactis was also tested (Gram-positive, naturally heme-free) but only Supplementary Fig. S4 qualitative growth inhibition, no numeric doubling time/growth rate -> no record.

growth_records.strain: K-12 JM108 (proline auxotroph); carrying 12.0 kbp pUC-origin plasmid, 53% GC, kanR (no kanamycin added)
growth_records.medium: Chemically defined glucose-minimal batch + linear glucose feed (non-sulfate-limited control)

up.method: Linear feed 4.45 g glucose/h (=320 g/L * 13.91 mL/h); qGlc=feed/biomass; at feed start X=1.51 g -> 4.45/1.51=2.95 g/gDW/h /180.16 g/mol =16.4 mmol/gDW/h. Glucose stayed below detection limit, confirming qGlc=feed rate. Decreases to ~4% (~0.66 mmol/gDW/h) by 23 h due to constant feed / rising biomass.

growth_records.cite: Gotsmy et al. Sulfate limitation increases specific plasmid DNA yield and productivity in E. coli fed-batch processes. Microbial Cell Factories (2023). DOI:10.1186/s12934-023-02248-2. [PMC10685491]
growth_records.snippet: control 57.1 +/- 0.7 | 2528 +/- 68 | 81.5 +/- 2.2 | 510 | 44.2 +/- 0.9 | 12.0 pUC None JM108 Lin. fed-batch (Table 2, all pDNA conformations, t=31 h). Glucose ... remained below the detection limit, indicating that cells consistently mainta
growth_records.notes: Control (non-sulfate-limited) linear fed-batch, E. coli JM108, n=3. Growth non-constant (linear feed => continuously decreasing mu). Feed phase ~5 generations in 35 h -> avg mu=5*ln2/35=0.099 h-1; doubling=7.0 h (approximation; instantaneous mu declines). Biomass: batch end 1.51 g CDM in 0.5 L (3.0 g/L) -> final 44.2 g/L (Table 2). Glucose uptake FEED-DERIVED: total glucose 48.5 g CDM /Y(0.303 g/g)=160 g over 0.5 L feed =320 g/L; *13.91 mL/h =4.45 g/h; qGlc(t)=4.45/X; feed-start (X=1.51 g) =2.95 g/gDW/h=16.4 mmol/gDW/h (glucose MW 180.16); glucose below detection confirms uptake=feed. Y_biomass/glucose=0.303 g/g is the design coefficient stated by authors. pDNA is an INTRACELLULAR product (not excreted) -> reported in yields, not secretion; specific yield 57.1 mg/g at 31 h (=0.0571 g/g). No acetate/organic-acid secretion reported (aerobic, DO>30%).

growth_records.strain: K-12 JM108 (proline auxotroph); carrying 12.0 kbp pUC-origin plasmid, 53% GC, kanR (no kanamycin added)
growth_records.medium: Chemically defined glucose-minimal, sulfate-limited (SLIM); initial SO4 3.8 g/L, sulfate-free feed

up.units: mmol/gDW/h (feed-start value; drops to ~0.66 mmol/gDW/h at 23 h)
up.method: Same linear feed 4.45 g glucose/h; qGlc=feed/biomass; feed-start 2.95 g/gDW/h=16.4 mmol/gDW/h; authors state specific glucose uptake had dropped to 4% of initial (~0.66 mmol/gDW/h) at onset of sulfate starvation (23 h). Glucose below detection throughout, so uptake=feed.

growth_records.cite: Gotsmy et al. Sulfate limitation increases specific plasmid DNA yield and productivity in E. coli fed-batch processes. Microbial Cell Factories (2023). DOI:10.1186/s12934-023-02248-2. [PMC10685491]
growth_records.snippet: SO4 2- limited 73.9 +/- 4.6 | 2788 +/- 161 | 89.9 +/- 5.2 | 660 | 38.2 +/- 1.0 | 12.0 pUC None JM108 Lin. fed-batch ... specific pDNA yield rises ... reaching a maximum of 0.074 g g-1 after 31 h ... improvement of 29% compared to control (p
growth_records.notes: Sulfate-limited (SLIM) linear fed-batch, E. coli JM108, n=6. Growth arrests upon SO4 starvation (~23 h); before that same linear-feed growth as control (avg mu~0.099 h-1, doubling 7.0 h over growing feed phase; declining). Final biomass 38.2 g/L (Table 2; lower than control 44.2 because growth stops at 23 h + dilution). Glucose uptake feed-derived as in control record: feed-start 16.4 mmol/gDW/h, dropping to ~4% (~0.66 mmol/gDW/h) at 23 h. Specific pDNA yield 73.9 mg/g (=0.074 g/g) at 31 h, +29% vs control; volumetric productivity 89.9 mg/L/h (+10%); plasmid copy number +29% (660 vs 510); supercoiled fraction +3% -> +33% supercoiled specific yield. pDNA is intracellular product (yields, not secretion). Y_biomass/glucose=0.303 g/g design value.

growth_records.snippet: At the 39-h mark, the cytidine yield for the original E. coli K12 strain was 1.58 ± 0.03 g/L. ... Cytidine fermentation was performed in an LB medium supplemented with glucose ... 90 mL of LB medium and 10 mL of glucose at 500 g/L ... 37 °C
growth_records.notes: Prokaryote (E. coli K12), fed-batch aerobic shake-flask cytidine fermentation. Initial glucose = 10 mL x 500 g/L / 100 mL = 50 g/L; two further 50 g/L-equivalent glucose pulses at 8 h and 24 h => fed-batch. Cytidine titer 1.58 +/- 0.03 g/L at 39 h (measured). Derived volumetric productivity = 1.58/39 = 0.0405 g/L/h (shown; NOT a specific mmol/gDW/h rate because no biomass/CDW reported). Acetate measured only as relative metabolomics abundance (this WT strain has the highest acetate; double mutant is 5.26-fold lower); no absolute acetate g/L or specific flux given. Growth followed as OD600 curve (Fig. 3A) but no numeric mu, doubling time, or CDW reported, so growth_rate/doubling_time null. pH monitored (declined during fermentation) but not controlled/set. LB base composition not enumerated in paper; only glucose supplement quantified, hence medium_composition_stated=false.

growth_records.cite: Dickinson et al. A system for the continuous directed evolution of proteases rapidly reveals drug-resistance mutations. Nature Communications (2014). DOI:10.1038/ncomms6352. [PMC4215169]
growth_records.snippet: Host cell culture chemostats containing 80 mL of Davis rich media were inoculated... At approximately OD600 1.0, fresh Davis rich media was pumped in at 80-100 mL h-1, with a chemostat waste needle set at 80 mL. This fixed dilution rate mai
growth_records.notes: E. coli S1030 host cells grown in a fixed-dilution-rate chemostat supplying PACE lagoons. Dilution rate D = flow/volume. 80 mL chemostat: 80-100 mL/h / 80 mL = 1.0-1.25 /h. 40 mL chemostat (HCV experiment): 50 mL/h / 40 mL = 1.25 /h. At chemostat steady state mu = D, so mu approx 1.0-1.25 /h; reported value 1.0 /h is the lower bound (80 mL/h into 80 mL). Doubling time = ln2/mu = 0.693/1.0 = 0.69 h (range 0.55-0.69 h across D=1.0-1.25). Text states culture held in 'late log phase' near OD600 1.0, so mu is near-maximal. Davis rich media composition not given in text (cited to ref 30). No substrate uptake or product secretion rates measured (paper is a directed-evolution/PACE study; the only quantitative physiology is the host-cell dilution/growth rate). pH not stated. Single record covers both chemostat configurations (same organism/strain/medium/temperature, same effective D approx 1.0-1.25 /h).

growth_records.cite: Wang et al. Programmable gene regulation for metabolic engineering using decoy transcription factor binding sites. Nucleic Acids Research (2021). DOI:10.1093/nar/gkaa1234. [PMC7826281]
growth_records.snippet: we observed an average titer of 790 uM arginine after co-transforming ArgA* and the ArgR decoy system, a 16-fold increase in arginine titer compared to the same strain without the decoy... even with the increase in arginine production, we o
growth_records.notes: E. coli BW25113 ArgA*+ArgR-decoy strain, batch M9+glucose, 37 C, 200 rpm, aerobic. Measured secreted product = L-arginine titer 790 uM by LC-MS after 24 h in 5 ml culture (n=3 biological replicates). This is an endpoint titer (concentration), not a biomass-normalized rate; specific uptake/secretion rates cannot be derived because no biomass (gDW/OD) or glucose-consumption data are reported. Growth: authors state 'no detectable growth differences compared with the wild type strain' (Fig 4C growth curves) but give no numeric mu; growth_rate left null. pH not stated. In 100 ml scale-up (Fig 4D) a 32-fold difference vs argR knockout was reported but absolute titers are only in the figure, not the text.
