growth_records.snippet: wild type E. coli cells cultured at a growth rate of 0.2, 0.5, and 0.7 hours-1
growth_records.notes: Growth rate 0.7 h-1 is an experimental E. coli value CITED from Ishii et al., 2007 [ref 34]. doubling_time = ln2/mu = 0.6931/0.7 = 0.990 h. At chemostat steady state mu = D so dilution_rate = 0.7 h-1. No uptake/secretion/MFA flux numbers reported in-text (validation fluxes only in supplementary S2 Dataset). Medium, temperature, pH not stated in this paper. NOTE: this paper also reused Holm et al. 2010 E. coli data (3 strains) and Ishii knockout mutants (Delta pgm/pgi/zwf/rpe/gapC) but gives NO growth rate or rate values for those in-text, so no records were created for them.

up.method: HPLC endpoint, 72 h anoxic batch (Table 1)

growth_records.cite: Park et al. Artificial consortium demonstrates emergent properties of enhanced cellulosic-sugar degradation and biofuel synthesis. NPJ Biofilms Microbiomes (2020). DOI:10.1038/s41522-020-00170-8. [PMC7710750]
growth_records.snippet: E. coli monocultures fermented 9.85 +/- 0.89 mM glycerol over 72 h; produced 16.66 acetate, 0.44 ethanol, 0.99 formate mM; biomass 0.090 g/L (Table 1).
growth_records.notes: Anoxic planktonic monoculture, mGS-2, 37 C, 72 h batch (Table 1). Values are net endpoint deltas (mM over 72 h), is_experimental true. E. coli did NOT catabolize cellobiose (no functional cellobiase). Biomass-specific rate [mmol/gDW/h] not derivable: growth is non-exponential and only endpoint CDW=0.090 g/L reported (conversion 1 OD600=0.45 gCDW/L). Crude average volumetric uptake = 9.85 mM /72 h = 0.137 mmol/L/h.

up.method: HPLC endpoint, 72 h anoxic batch (Table 1)
HPLC endpoint (Table 1)

growth_records.cite: Park et al. Artificial consortium demonstrates emergent properties of enhanced cellulosic-sugar degradation and biofuel synthesis. NPJ Biofilms Microbiomes (2020). DOI:10.1038/s41522-020-00170-8. [PMC7710750]
growth_records.snippet: The consortium consumed 8.84 mM cellobiose and 19.89 mM glycerol; produced 26.71 mM ethanol, 25.40 mM acetate, 7.49 mM formate; biomass 0.315 g/L; pH 5.8 (Table 1).
growth_records.notes: Anoxic planktonic binary co-culture (E. coli + C. phytofermentans), mGS-2, 37 C, 72 h batch (Table 1). Net endpoint deltas (mM/72 h). Emergent: +240% cellobiose catabolism, +102% glycerol, +837% ethanol, +121% biomass vs sum of monocultures. No succinate detected. Biomass-specific rate not derivable (endpoint CDW=0.315 g/L only). Crude average volumetric rates: cellobiose 8.84/72=0.123, glycerol 19.89/72=0.276 mmol/L/h.

up.method: measured (mean of triplicates, std 0.24); reproduced in Table 2

growth_records.cite: von Kamp, Axel et al. Balancing biomass reaction stoichiometry and measured fluxes in flux balance analysis. Bioinformatics (2023). DOI:10.1093/bioinformatics/btad600. [PMC10568370]
growth_records.snippet: Table 2. Reported measured fluxes in E.coli under anaerobic conditions (Monk et al. 2016) ... Glucose uptake 16.69 (0.24) Ethanol excretion 11.22 (0.6) Acetate excretion 11.71 (1.14) Lactate excretion 0 (0) Formate excretion 22.17 (1.69) Su
growth_records.notes: Rates are experimental measurements (mean +/- SD of triplicates) reproduced verbatim in Table 2 of this FBA-methods paper; they are CITED FROM Monk JM et al. (2016) 'Multi-omics quantification of species variation of Escherichia coli' Cell Syst 3:238-251 (original source of measured anaerobic MG1655 fluxes). Flux units mmol/(gDW h), growth rate 1/h per table footnote. Doubling time computed td=ln2/mu=0.693/0.46=1.507 h. GAM 75.38 mmol/gDW is an iML1515 model parameter, not an experimental physiology rate. Temperature/pH/medium composition not given in this paper.

up.method: measured (std 0.04); Table 3
measured (std n/a); Table 3

growth_records.cite: von Kamp, Axel et al. Balancing biomass reaction stoichiometry and measured fluxes in flux balance analysis. Bioinformatics (2023). DOI:10.1093/bioinformatics/btad600. [PMC10568370]
growth_records.snippet: Table 3. Measured fluxes in E.coli under anaerobic conditions reported in Boecker et al. (2021) ... Glucose uptake 13.48 (0.04) NH4 uptake 6.52 (n/a) Ethanol excretion 10.81 (0.14) Acetate excretion 11.90 (0.19) Lactate excretion 0.83 (0.05
growth_records.notes: Experimental measured fluxes (mean +/- SD) reproduced in Table 3; CITED FROM Boecker S, Slaviero G, Schramm T et al. (2021) 'Deciphering the physiological response of Escherichia coli under high ATP demand' Mol Syst Biol 17:e10504 (original source). Units mmol/(gDW h); growth rate 1/h per table footnote. Doubling time td=ln2/mu=0.693/0.476=1.456 h. Paper notes these measured rates carry a carbon shortage of 15.53 mmol/(gDW h) and nitrogen excess of 1.38 mmol/(gDW h). Model GAM 31.578 mmol/gDW is an ECC2/iJO1366 parameter, not experimental. Temperature/pH/medium not given here.

growth_records.strain: K-12 MG1655 (lac-, rpoS-, lambda-cured, plasmid-free ancestor)
growth_records.cite: Warsi, Omar M. et al. Different adaptive strategies in E. coli populations evolving under macronutrient limitation and metal ion limitation. BMC Evolutionary Biology (2018). DOI:10.1186/s12862-018-1191-4. [PMC5960147]
growth_records.snippet: For nitrogen starvation experiments ammonium ion was used at a concentration of 0.05 g/L (0.7 mM). Sodium sulphate was used to compensate for sulphate concentrations (0.9 g/L)... The dilution rate of the chemostat was maintained at 0.33/h,
growth_records.notes: Nitrogen-limited continuous chemostat of E. coli at steady state, so mu = dilution rate D = 0.33/h (reported). Doubling time td=ln2/mu=0.693/0.33=2.10 h (paper states ~2 h generation time, consistent). Base M9 stated: 1.75 g/L K2HPO4, 0.5 g/L KH2PO4, 1 g/L (NH4)2SO4 [reduced here for N-limitation], 0.5 g/L sodium citrate, 0.1 g/L MgSO4, 1 g/L glucose; N-limitation sets ammonium to 0.05 g/L (0.7 mM) with 0.9 g/L Na2SO4 added to keep sulphate constant. No substrate uptake or product secretion rates were measured in this experimental-evolution study. growth_method=calculated (mu inferred from steady-state D=mu).

growth_records.strain: K-12 MG1655 (lac-, rpoS-, lambda-cured, plasmid-free ancestor)
growth_records.cite: Warsi, Omar M. et al. Different adaptive strategies in E. coli populations evolving under macronutrient limitation and metal ion limitation. BMC Evolutionary Biology (2018). DOI:10.1186/s12862-018-1191-4. [PMC5960147]
growth_records.snippet: For magnesium ion starvation experiments, no magnesium sulphate was added in the media... The dilution rate of the chemostat was maintained at 0.33/h, which results in a ~ 2 h generation time.
growth_records.notes: Magnesium-limited continuous chemostat of E. coli at steady state, so mu = D = 0.33/h (reported). Doubling time td=ln2/0.33=2.10 h (paper: ~2 h generation time). Same base M9 as the N-limited condition (1.75 g/L K2HPO4, 0.5 g/L KH2PO4, 1 g/L (NH4)2SO4, 0.5 g/L sodium citrate, 1 g/L glucose) but MgSO4 omitted to make Mg growth-limiting. No uptake/secretion rates measured. growth_method=calculated (mu inferred from steady-state D=mu).

Fluxes are normalized to glucose uptake rate (glucupt); absolute mmol/gDW/h values not restated in this paper's text (in figures/S3 Text). WT E. coli mu ~0.83-0.9 /h mentioned as a general range. No NEW experimental rate generated here; MFA fluxes are relative/normalized.

growth_records.cite: Vargas-Tah A et al. Production of cinnamic and p-hydroxycinnamic acid from sugar mixtures with engineered Escherichia coli. Microbial Cell Factories (2015). DOI:10.1186/s12934-014-0185-1. [PMC4305220]
growth_records.snippet: Production of CA was improved in strains derived from W3110 when comparing xylose to glucose as a carbon source, with strain WPJAt displaying the highest observed titer (736.8 μmol/L), corresponding to a 1.4 fold improvement over glucose.
growth_records.notes: W3110 (PTS+) + A. thaliana PAL on xylose. CA titer 736.8 µM, 1.4-fold over glucose; YCA/Xyl and titer ~2-fold higher than VPJAt on xylose. qS/μ in Additional file 2.

up.method: consumed (in SH; glucose causes catabolite repression of xylose/arabinose in W3110 strains); rate not quantified
main carbon source in SH; consumed after glucose (sequential in W3110)
consumed; partially repressed by glucose
HPLC; acetate NOT consumed by any strain, concentration increased over culture

sec.method: HPLC; QpHCA = 22.24 µmol/L/h (Table 2); 8-fold increase in YpHCA/SH from pheA inactivation

growth_records.cite: Vargas-Tah A et al. Production of cinnamic and p-hydroxycinnamic acid from sugar mixtures with engineered Escherichia coli. Microbial Cell Factories (2015). DOI:10.1186/s12934-014-0185-1. [PMC4305220]
growth_records.snippet: The SH contained xylose (53.28 mM), glucose (6.66 mM), arabinose (5.33) and acetic acid (6.09 mM). ... Strain W(pheA−)Rg displayed an 8-fold increase in YpHCA/SH ... acetate in the SH was not consumed by any of the four strains, on the cont
growth_records.notes: pheA− mutant + R. glutinis PAL on simulated hydrolysate (SH). pHCA 355.87 µM (QpHCA 22.24 µmol/L/h), CA 105.45 µM (QCA 6.59 µmol/L/h) at 16 h (Table 2). pheA inactivation gave 8-fold higher YpHCA/SH. IMPORTANT experimental uptake finding: acetate (6.09 mM in SH) was NOT consumed by any of the 4 strains and was instead net-produced (concentration increased) — acetate secretion. SH sugar concentrations: methods state xylose 58.28 mM but Results text states 53.28 mM (discrepancy noted); glucose 6.66, arabinose 5.33 mM. W3110-derived strains show sequential (glucose-repressed) sugar consumption; qS values in Additional file 2.

up.method: consumed first (catabolite repression of xylose/arabinose)
consumed after glucose
HPLC

growth_records.cite: Vargas-Tah A et al. Production of cinnamic and p-hydroxycinnamic acid from sugar mixtures with engineered Escherichia coli. Microbial Cell Factories (2015). DOI:10.1186/s12934-014-0185-1. [PMC4305220]
growth_records.snippet: WPJAt | A. thaliana | SH | M9 | 31 h | - | - | 377.87 [CA µM] | 12.19 [Q]. ... When compared to results from W(pheA−)At cultures, the YCA/SH and qCA of WPJAt were [higher].
growth_records.notes: W3110 (PTS+) + A. thaliana PAL on SH. CA titer 377.87 µM, QCA = 12.19 µmol/L/h at 31 h (Table 2). Sequential glucose-repressed sugar utilization; acetate net-produced (not consumed). qS/μ in Additional file 2.

up.method: consumed as carbon source; no numeric uptake rate given

growth_records.cite: Wang, Guanglu et al. Recent progress in adaptive laboratory evolution of industrial microorganisms. Journal of Industrial Microbiology & Biotechnology (2022). DOI:10.1093/jimb/kuac023. [PMC9936214]
growth_records.snippet: The parental strain could not grow in acetate-containing medium, but the evolved strain had a maximum growth rate of 0.061 ± 0.001 h−1.
growth_records.notes: CITED in review FROM Seong et al. 2020. Evolved E. coli DSM01 max growth rate 0.061±0.001 h-1 on sodium acetate. doubling_time = ln2/0.061 = 11.36 h. No numeric substrate uptake/secretion rate given. Review paper (secondary).

up.method: glycerol utilization increased 46.8% (relative); no absolute rate given

growth_records.cite: Wang, Guanglu et al. Recent progress in adaptive laboratory evolution of industrial microorganisms. Journal of Industrial Microbiology & Biotechnology (2022). DOI:10.1093/jimb/kuac023. [PMC9936214]
growth_records.snippet: its glycerol utilization increased by 46.8%, while the growth rate increased by nearly 40%, from 0.67 ± 0.03 to 0.94 ± 0.01 h-1
growth_records.notes: CITED in review FROM Kim et al. 2022. Evolved E. coli CY02 growth rate 0.94±0.01 h-1 (parent 0.67±0.03) on M9+0.2% glycerol. doubling_time = ln2/0.94 = 0.737 h. Glycerol utilization +46.8% (no absolute uptake rate). Review paper (secondary).

growth_records.cite: Wang, Guanglu et al. Recent progress in adaptive laboratory evolution of industrial microorganisms. Journal of Industrial Microbiology & Biotechnology (2022). DOI:10.1093/jimb/kuac023. [PMC9936214]
growth_records.snippet: This evolved strain could produce 38.77 g/L of tryptophan with a yield of 0.164 g/g. ... Continuous mutagenesis was performed using CGSS at 30°C.
growth_records.notes: CITED in review FROM Chen et al. 2021. E. coli S028 tryptophan titer 38.77 g/L, product yield 0.164 g/g substrate, 30°C. Titer not a rate (no fermentation time given so no productivity derivable). Review paper (secondary).

sec.method: derived from titer 25.42 g/L at 88 h

growth_records.cite: Wang, Guanglu et al. Recent progress in adaptive laboratory evolution of industrial microorganisms. Journal of Industrial Microbiology & Biotechnology (2022). DOI:10.1093/jimb/kuac023. [PMC9936214]
growth_records.snippet: The 4HPAA titer was 25.42 g/L at 88 h, which was 127.3% higher than that of the parental strain.
growth_records.notes: CITED in review FROM Shen et al. 2022. 4HPAA titer 25.42 g/L at 88 h. Derived mean volumetric productivity = 25.42 g/L / 88 h = 0.289 g/L/h (arithmetic shown; assumes linear accumulation, average not instantaneous). Review paper (secondary).

growth_records.snippet: Table 1: aerobic chemostat, D=0.2/h, pH 7.0, wild type/dphoB/dphoR E. coli under different phosphate concentrations; specific glucose uptake and acetate production rates in mmol/gDCW/h.
growth_records.notes: Chemostat steady state: growth_rate mu = D = 0.2 /h (glucose non-limiting at D=0.2). doubling_time = ln2/mu = 0.6931/0.2 = 3.466 h. Reported specific glucose uptake rate qS_glc = 2.69 mmol/gDCW/h and specific acetate production rate = 0.04 mmol/gDCW/h (Table, triplicate +/- SD). Steady-state biomass = 3.86 g/L, residual glucose = 0.66 g/L, acetate = 0.046 g/L. Yxs computed = mu/(qS_glc*0.18015 g/mmol) = 0.2/(2.69*0.18015) = 0.413 gDW/g-glucose. Acetate/glucose yield = qAc/qS_glc = 0.04/2.69 = 0.015 mol/mol.

growth_records.snippet: Fluxes were predicted ... scaled to 0.0856 h−1 to resemble the growth rate of MEcoli_ref_1 (Fig. 3b). ... grows at a doubling time of about 8 h.
growth_records.notes: Experimental specific growth rate of evolved methylotroph MEcoli_ref_1 = 0.0856 h-1 (stated as the measured growth rate used to scale FBA in Fig 3b); consistent with reported doubling time ~8 h. Check: td = ln2/mu = 0.6931/0.0856 = 8.10 h. All methanol uptake rates in the paper (0.6, 0.597, 9.119, 100 mmol gCDW-1 h-1) are FBA/FVA MODEL constraints or predictions, NOT experimental measurements, so not entered as experimental uptake. 13C tracer work gives isotopologue/fractional-contribution data (biomass built entirely from methanol) but no net flux magnitudes, so mfa_fluxes left empty. Growth on methanol as sole carbon and energy source demonstrated experimentally.

growth_records.snippet: When we inoculated the population in a shake flask with medium containing only methanol (500 mM) as a carbon source, the culture was indeed able to grow, reaching an optical density of about 0.4 at a doubling time (Td) of 60 h (Fig. 3a).
growth_records.notes: First demonstration of growth on methanol alone by the evolved population (249 generations), doubling time Td = 60 h (reported/measured from OD). Growth rate calculated: mu = ln2/td = 0.6931/60 = 0.01155 h-1. IPTG no longer required at this stage.

growth_records.snippet: The dilution rates were 0.42, 0.34, and 0.62 d−1 for reactor 1, 2, and 3, respectively. ... The strain was evolved under continuous conditions in a chemostat in which the dilution rate of the culture defined its growth rate.
growth_records.notes: Chemostat evolution on methanol+pyruvate co-substrate. At steady state mu = D. Reported dilution rates: reactor 1 = 0.42 d-1, reactor 2 = 0.34 d-1, reactor 3 = 0.62 d-1 (reactor restarts at gen 200 and 223). Converted: 0.42/24 = 0.0175 h-1 (reactor 1, used here); reactor 2 = 0.34/24 = 0.0142 h-1; reactor 3 = 0.62/24 = 0.0258 h-1. Doubling time reactor 1 = ln2/0.0175 = 39.6 h. Growth rate is the applied dilution rate (paper explicitly states dilution rate defined growth rate).

growth_records.snippet: The experiment in which the limiting substrate was changed from glucose to ammonia was conducted at a dilution rate of 0.155 h-1 ... In both experiments, the steady state value of oxygen consumption and carbon dioxide production is around 1
growth_records.notes: E. coli MG1655 aerobic glucose-limited chemostat steady state; mu = D = 0.155 /h at steady state; td = ln2/0.155 = 4.47 h. Full central-carbon net fluxes are DMFA-derived but presented only as figure fluxmaps (Figures 8-9, units mol/mol Biomass/h) with no in-text numeric table. Steady-state O2 uptake and CO2 production both ~16 g/mol Biomass/h (verbatim paper unit). Model: 136 reactions/150 metabolites, 10 measured exchanges (CO2, O2, NH3, PiOH, acetate, lactate, pyruvate, succinate, glucose, biomass). Culture temperature not stated (procedures in ref [25] De Mey et al. 2007 Biotechnol Prog 23:1053). Max E. coli growth rate quoted as 0.7 /h (general). Transient (non-steady-state) growth rates observed up to ~0.6 /h; transient lag-phase ATP maintenance ~0.6 mol ATP/mol Biom/h - dynamic, not a steady-state condition.

growth_records.strain: PFM2 (MG1655 derivative with repaired rpoS and rph)

up.method: 10 mM glucose fermented; consumption qualitative (no gDW-normalized rate in text)

sec.method: fermentation products, cross-fed to R. palustris; qualitative

growth_records.cite: Chuang, Ying-Chih et al. Reciprocal cross-feeding between bacteria can limit the emergence of metabolic dependencies. Applied and Environmental Microbiology (2026). DOI:10.1128/aem.00363-26. [PMC13274399]
growth_records.snippet: E. coli PFM2 was fed 10 mM NH4Cl and 10 mM glucose, which it fermented to a mixture of OAcs, ethanol, CO2, and H2... All cultures were grown horizontally without shaking at 30C with light in MDC.
growth_records.notes: Anaerobic fermentative E. coli monoculture on defined M9-derived MDC (glucose + NH4Cl stated). Fermentation products (organic acids, ethanol, CO2, H2) reported qualitatively. Growth measured by OD660 (Genesys 20), harvested 0.6-0.8 OD660; numeric growth rates appear only in figure bar charts (not extractable from XML), so mu/td left null. This is an experimental-evolution paper, not a physiology-rate paper; rates are qualitative. Glucose kept at 10 mM in monoculture to avoid lethal acidification.

growth_records.cite: Forsyth VS et al. Rapid Growth of Uropathogenic Escherichia coli during Human Urinary Tract Infection. mBio (2018). DOI:10.1128/mBio.00186-18. [PMC5844997]
growth_records.snippet: a low PTR value was calculated for bacteria growing in minimal medium (M9 salts supplemented with 0.4% glucose) (PTR = 1.27, 54.2-min doubling time)
growth_records.notes: Doubling time 54.2 min reported directly. mu = ln2/td = 0.693/(54.2/60 h) = 0.693/0.9033 = 0.767 /h; doubling_time_h = 54.2/60 = 0.903 h. Temperature 37C is the lab standard stated elsewhere in the paper (urine cultures at 37C with aeration; Fig 2 LB growth curves at 37C with aeration); Fig 3 media paragraph itself states 'cultured with aeration'. No uptake/secretion rates reported. NOTE: separate LB PTR measurement for the same strain is a distinct record below.

growth_records.cite: Forsyth VS et al. Rapid Growth of Uropathogenic Escherichia coli during Human Urinary Tract Infection. mBio (2018). DOI:10.1128/mBio.00186-18. [PMC5844997]
growth_records.snippet: High PTR values were calculated for bacteria growing in rich media (LB medium and Terrific broth) (PTR = 1.62, 40.5-min doubling time, and PTR = 1.68, 40.0-min doubling time, respectively)
growth_records.notes: Doubling time 40.5 min reported (PTR method). mu = ln2/td = 0.693/(40.5/60) = 0.693/0.675 = 1.027 /h; doubling_time_h = 40.5/60 = 0.675 h. 37C aerobic per paper's standard (Fig 2 LB growth at 37C with aeration). This PTR-derived LB doubling (40.5 min) differs from the Table 1 in-vitro LB doubling for CFT073 (32.0 min) — separate experiments/methods; both retained as distinct records.

growth_records.cite: Forsyth VS et al. Rapid Growth of Uropathogenic Escherichia coli during Human Urinary Tract Infection. mBio (2018). DOI:10.1128/mBio.00186-18. [PMC5844997]
growth_records.snippet: rich media (LB medium and Terrific broth) (PTR = 1.62, 40.5-min doubling time, and PTR = 1.68, 40.0-min doubling time, respectively)
growth_records.notes: Doubling time 40.0 min reported (PTR method). mu = ln2/td = 0.693/(40.0/60) = 0.693/0.6667 = 1.040 /h; doubling_time_h = 40.0/60 = 0.667 h. 37C aerobic per paper standard. No uptake/secretion rates in paper.

growth_records.snippet: Methanol consumed (g/L) X1 0.58 +/- 0.03 ... Methanol consumption rate (g/L*OD-1) X1 1.01 +/- 0.01. strain X1 showed 10.90 +/- 0.01% higher final biomass concentration compared to that without methanol; strains X1 and X2 accumulated 35.66 +
growth_records.notes: Table 2 (48 h). Methanol consumed 0.58+/-0.03 g/L; normalized consumption rate 1.01+/-0.01 g/L*OD-1; final OD600 with MeOH 0.57+/-0.01, without MeOH 0.52+/-0.01; biomass increase by methanol = (0.57-0.52)/0.52*100 = 10.9% (Eq.1, matches paper 10.90%). No specific growth rate or doubling time reported for this strain (the ~8 h doubling time in the Introduction refers to Chen 2020 and Keller 2022 RuMP strains, NOT this paper). Formaldehyde is a pathway intermediate accumulating in supernatant, not a true secretion product. qMeOH in mmol/gDW/h not derived: biomass reported only as OD600, no gDW conversion given. Methanol MW=32.04, so 0.58 g/L = 18.1 mmol/L consumed over 48 h.

Clark oxygen electrode, dissolved-oxygen time-course

yields.note: ATP/O2 = 5.74 is model-derived (stoichiometric), not a directly measured yield

growth_records.cite: Yijie Deng et al. Measuring and modeling energy and power consumption in living microbial cells with a synthetic ATP reporter. BMC Biology (2021). DOI:10.1186/s12915-021-01023-2. [PMC8130387]
growth_records.snippet: the specific growth rates are determined by Monod equations where mu max,S and mu max,A are the experimentally measured maximal specific growth rates on glucose and acetate ... Increasing the specific growth rate from 0.4 to 0.7 (1/h)
growth_records.notes: Experimentally measured maximal specific growth rate on glucose for the Fig.6 batch = 0.4 /h (baseline of the growth-rate sweep; the 0.7/h endpoint is a model sensitivity sweep, NOT a second measurement). doubling_time = ln2/mu = 0.693/0.4 = 1.73 h (calculated). Glucose consumption, acetate production/re-consumption and dissolved-O2 were all MEASURED as time-courses (Fig.6b-d) and fitted with the kinetic model, but no single tabulated qS/qP/qO2 in mmol/gDW/h is given in the text (only in figures), so uptake/secretion rates left null. Model-derived (not experimental) energetics: total ATP production ~6.4 million ATP/cell/s in exponential phase, ~0.82 (lag) and ~0.23 (stationary) million ATP/cell/s; ATP/O2 = 5.74. Medium = MOPS minimal defined medium (TEKnova M2106) with 10 mM glucose; full salt recipe not enumerated in text so medium_composition_stated=false. Final culture pH kept above 7.3.

growth_records.snippet: Two dilution rates (0.013 and 0.0043 h-1) were tested using a glucose concentration of 0.55 g/L on the feed stream... Glucose consumption values were obtained from a mass balance, by multiplying the glucose concentration difference (feed mi
growth_records.notes: Higher-dilution-rate condition (D=0.013 h-1). Growth rate NOT set equal to D: authors explicitly state 'the higher dilution rate may exceed the maximum growth rate of planktonic cells... washout is not observed due to the dynamic process of biofilm detachment and sloughing' -> steady-state mu=D assumption invalid in this biofilm system. Glucose uptake measured experimentally (DNS mass balance) but numeric values reside only in Figure 7b (units g/h, whole-system, not per-gDW), so no rate extracted. Medium pH 7.0, 30C stated in Methods.

growth_records.snippet: a recirculating tank of 5 L (corresponding to a total system volume of 5.92 L) was used to obtain a dilution rate of 0.0043 h-1... the lower dilution rate favored planktonic growth and biofilm thickness.
growth_records.notes: Lower-dilution-rate condition (D=0.0043 h-1). mu not equated to D (biofilm sloughing decouples planktonic growth from dilution). Glucose consumption higher than the high-D condition after day 9 (Fig 7b) but only figure data; no numeric per-gDW uptake extractable. doubling_time not computed because mu is undefined here.

growth_records.snippet: The E. coli K-12 strain BW2952 ... was used as the ancestor to initiate glucose-limited chemostats that were fed with 0.02% wt/vol glucose in minimal medium A with a 0.1 h-1 dilution rate. ... Acetate production ... -, below the detection l
growth_records.notes: Chemostat at steady state: mu = D = 0.1 /h (Table 4 legend / Methods). Doubling time td = ln2/mu = 0.6931/0.1 = 6.93 h. Growth yield reported RELATIVE to ancestor (=1.00, from OD600 of 24-h glucose-limited chemostats), so absolute Yxs and absolute biomass are NOT available; therefore glucose uptake and acetate secretion cannot be converted to mmol/gDW/h (no absolute CDW). Acetate 'production' is reported as a residual effluent concentration category (ancestor '-' = <0.15 mg/L), not a rate. Temperature not explicitly stated for the chemostat (37 C used for LB precultures/incubations). pH not stated. Full composition of 'minimal medium A' is cited (ref [48]) not given in this paper. Table 3 + Methods.

growth_records.cite: Karlsen E et al. A study of a diauxic growth experiment using an expanded dynamic flux balance framework. PLOS ONE (2023). DOI:10.1371/journal.pone.0280077. [PMC9821518]
growth_records.method: reported (measured from exponential-phase OD600; mu=0.66 h-1 stated). Doubling time calculated: ln2/0.66=0.693/0.66=1.05 h
growth_records.snippet: During the exponential growth phases, growth rates were measured to 0.66 h-1, while the terminal biomass values were measured to 8.2 gCDW L-1.
growth_records.notes: Experimental measured values (priority): mu=0.66 h-1 (exponential phase), terminal biomass 8.2 gCDW/L. Doubling time computed mu=ln2/td => td=ln2/0.66=1.05 h. Glucose (10.5) and O2 (15) uptake and acetate (2.2) excretion are decFBA model constraints tuned to reproduce the fermentation, NOT direct rate measurements, hence is_experimental=false; raw fermentor/off-gas data are in S4 File. Medium is glucose minimal M9 (recipe in S1 File, not in main text) => medium_composition_stated=false. Acetate excretion was experimentally observed as the sole appreciable by-product but its measured rate not tabulated in text. pH held >=7 by NaOH addition; 37 C; 40% DO.

growth_records.cite: Rodrigo García et al. Bacteriophage Production Models: An Overview. Frontiers in Microbiology (2019). DOI:10.3389/fmicb.2019.01187. [PMC6558064]
growth_records.snippet: Escherichia coli K12 MG1655 - Phage T4 (DSM 4505) Phage productivity: 1x10 9 PFU mL -1 h -1 Production: 2.4x10 13 PFU day -1 Working Volume: 1 L Dilution rate : 2.0 h -1 Nabergoj et al.,2018a
growth_records.notes: CITED value, not measured in this review (a phage-production model overview). Table 2 reports a continuous 'cellstat' E. coli K-12 MG1655 phage-T4 production run with dilution rate D = 2.0 h-1 (from Nabergoj et al., 2018a). At chemostat/cellstat steady state mu = D = 2.0 h-1; doubling_time = ln2/2.0 = 0.347 h (~21 min). No medium composition, temperature, pH, or substrate-uptake/product-secretion rates are given in this review (only phage titres, PFU-based). Included as a growth-rate parameter with clear provenance to the primary source.

growth_records.snippet: For the Gly medium, cell growth was delayed and reached an OD660 of 1.8 at 10 h and continued proliferation to reach an OD660 of 8.6 at 25 h... Glycerol was consumed completely by 20 h in Gly medium.
growth_records.notes: Lower IPTG (10 µM) strongly improves glycerol growth vs 100 µM (final OD660 8.6 vs 1.1). Delayed growth: OD660 0.2 (0h) to 1.8 (10h) to 8.6 (25h). Recorded mu is the overall 0-25 h average = ln(8.6/0.2)/25 = ln(43)/25 = 3.761/25 = 0.150 h-1 (approximate, includes lag/delay); td=ln2/0.150=4.62 h. Post-lag phase (10-25 h) mu approx = ln(8.6/1.8)/15 = ln(4.778)/15 = 0.104 h-1. Exponential-phase mu not cleanly separable; treat as approximate. Glycerol volumetric uptake 8 g/L / 20 h = 0.4 g/L/h. Biomass-specific rates not computable (OD only).

growth_records.cite: Ibacache-Quiroga, Claudia et al. Parallel Evolution of High-Level Aminoglycoside Resistance in Escherichia coli Under Low and High Mutation Supply Rates. Frontiers in Microbiology (2018). DOI:10.3389/fmicb.2018.00427. [PMC5867336]
growth_records.snippet: Chemostats were continuously fed with fresh culture medium with gentamicin at a dilution rate of 0.24 h-1, and maintained at 37C and aeration of 1.2 l/h. ... M9 minimal medium with glucose as only carbon and energy source.
growth_records.notes: E. coli = prokaryote. At chemostat steady state specific growth rate mu = dilution rate D = 0.24 h-1 (computed, mu=D). Doubling time td=ln2/mu=0.693/0.24=2.89 h (computed). Glucose 0.2% (w/v) = 2 g/L; full M9 salt composition not enumerated in text (medium_composition_stated:false). Chemostat was an evolution device with gentamicin doubled every 72 h; this record captures the ancestor at the initial near-sub-inhibitory stage (gentamicin 0.03 ug/mL, 0.125x MIC of 0.25 ug/mL). No substrate uptake, product secretion, or MFA fluxes reported; batch growth curves give only RELATIVE growth rates (ancestor=1.00), so no absolute batch mu extractable.

growth_records.notes: Aerobic co-culture (Fig 3). Both sugars consumed simultaneously/independently; volumetric rates = delta-S/time. Final OD ~25 = sum of the two single-strain ODs (10+15), indicating additive independent growth. ~15% faster than single-organism diauxic process.

growth_records.snippet: During the entire fed-batch process, the xylose and glucose concentrations in the fermenter remained at zero... the distribution of the microbial population responded in unison with the shift in substrate concentrations.
growth_records.notes: Aerobic fed-batch (Fig 4). mu set at 0.1/h via exponential feed (far below max growth rate). Population self-adjusted: ZSC113 fraction 35% (20:30) ->50% (30:30) ->42% (30:60) ->32% (20:60), tracking xylose:glucose feed ratio. Sugars held at zero => each substrate individually limiting. Per-strain specific uptake not tabulated numerically.

growth_records.notes: Anaerobic mixed-acid (Fig 5), single WT strain OD 21. qGlc 630 mg/gh -> 630/180.16 = 3.497 mmol/gDW/h. qXyl 310 mg/gh -> 310/150.13 = 2.065 mmol/gDW/h initial, falling <150 mg/gh (<1.0 mmol/gDW/h) after 4 h (attributed to acetate inhibition of xylose uptake). Products qualitatively measured; formate highest, lactate lowest.

growth_records.snippet: grown in the chemostat as a batch culture for 15-17 h (~10 generations) with 1.1 l/min aeration, at 500 rpm ... at 37C ... grown for an additional 5 days (up to 100 generations) at a dilution rate of 0.5 per h, using the same medium
growth_records.notes: Glucose-limited chemostat competition at imposed D=0.5/h => steady-state mu=D=0.5/h (calculated). td=ln2/0.5=1.386 h. Medium M9 + 0.2 m/m% glucose + 0.03 m/m% PEG6000. No uptake/secretion rates measured. Batch LB doubling/lag times were measured but reported only as box-plot figures (no numeric values in text), so not captured as separate records.

growth_records.snippet: For the minimal media no limitation batch cultures, NCM3722 cells were grown at 37 C in 40 mM MOPS media with glucose (0.4%), ammonium (9.5 mM) and phosphate (1.32 mM)... grown up to an OD ~0.4
growth_records.notes: Growth rate calculated from reported 42-min doubling time: mu = ln2/(42/60 h) = 0.693/0.700 = 0.990 h^-1; doubling_time_h = 0.70. No substrate uptake or product secretion rates reported (protein-turnover study). pH taken as MOPS-buffered 7.2 (chemostat pH held 7.2+/-0.1; batch pH not separately stated). Glucose 0.4% w/v = 4 g/L ~= 22.2 mM.

growth_records.snippet: continuous culturing (switch-on of the pump) was started at a dilution rate of 0.25 h-1. Samples were harvested at steady state growth ... We did not detect any residual glucose or amino acid concentrations in supernatants
growth_records.notes: Chemostat steady state: mu = dilution rate D = 0.25 h-1 (by definition). Doubling time td = ln2/0.25 = 2.77 h. Single growth rate imposed across all 10 clinical isolates and +/- ertapenem to standardize physiology, so one record. Glucose and casamino acids fully consumed (no residual), but no quantitative qS given. Reactor temperature/pH not stated in text (MIC assays were at 37C, not necessarily the chemostat). No named type strain.

growth_records.cite: Alexei Vazquez et al. Impact of the solvent capacity constraint on E. coli metabolism. BMC Systems Biology (2008). DOI:10.1186/1752-0509-2-7. [PMC2270259]
growth_records.snippet: Cells were grown in a continuous growth mode at 5 different dilution rates (0.1, 0.25, 0.4, 0.55, and 0.72 L h-1) in a Labfors bioreactor... The experimental flux measurements were performed at dilution rates 0.1, 0.25, 0.4, 0.55 and 0.72 h
growth_records.notes: Steady-state chemostat: mu = dilution rate = 0.1 h-1. Doubling time = ln2/mu = 0.6931/0.1 = 6.93 h. 13C-MFA (10% [1,2-13C2]-glucose) measured central-carbon net fluxes (glycolysis, zwf/PPP, TCA cycle, acetate excretion, ppc, aceE) at this dilution rate (Fig. 3, blue plots), but numeric measured flux values, glucose uptake, and acetate excretion rates are presented only in Fig. 3 / Additional file 5 and are NOT given numerically in the fulltext, so uptake_rates/secretion_rates/mfa_fluxes left empty (no invented numbers). Predicted (not measured) FBAwMC maximum glucose uptake ~15 mmol/g/h and max mu ~0.7 h-1 are stated as within range of experimentally determined values cited from ref [18], not this study's measurement.

growth_records.notes: Steady-state chemostat: mu = D = 0.25 h-1. Doubling time = ln2/mu = 0.6931/0.25 = 2.77 h. 13C-MFA central-carbon net fluxes measured at this D (Fig. 3); numeric measured flux/uptake/secretion values only in Fig. 3 / Additional file 5, not in fulltext, hence rate arrays empty.

growth_records.strain: BW25113 dysregulated citrulline producer (delta-argG delta-argR argA_H15Y) expressing temperature-sensitive ArgG variant G9 from plasmid pTS036

up.method: glucose assay (GOPOD) of supernatant, during first 5 h of 39C stationary/production phase

sec.method: LC-MS/MS of whole broth, constant over 30 h 39C stationary phase

growth_records.cite: Schramm et al. High-throughput enrichment of temperature-sensitive argininosuccinate synthetase for two-stage citrulline production in E. coli. Metabolic Engineering (2020). DOI:10.1016/j.ymben.2020.03.004. [PMC7225747]
growth_records.snippet: the cells grew exponentially with growth rates of 0.31 h-1 and 0.35 h-1 ... specific glucose uptake rates were 4.2 mmol gDW-1 h-1 and 5.3 mmol gDW-1 h-1 ... specific citrulline production was constant at 0.92 mmol gDW-1 h-1 (bioreactor 1) a
growth_records.notes: Two-stage bioreactor 1. Growth rate 0.31 h-1 measured during exponential 33C growth phase (reported). Doubling time = ln2/0.31 = 2.236 h. Glucose uptake (4.2 mmol/gDW/h) and citrulline production (0.92 mmol/gDW/h) measured during 39C production phase; OD-to-DW factor 0.37 gDW/OD/L. Final titer 3.3 g/L, biomass-specific yield 4.88 g/gDW (reported Suppl. Table 3). Paper notes these uptake rates correspond to ~45% of glucose uptake of glucose-fed wild-type E. coli at 37C exponential growth cited from Long et al. 2018 (reference value not numerically stated).

growth_records.strain: delta-argG delta-argR argA_H15Y (doubly dysregulated, no ArgG; non-growing citrulline producer)

sec.method: LC-MS/MS whole broth, regression over first 10 h

growth_records.cite: Schramm et al. High-throughput enrichment of temperature-sensitive argininosuccinate synthetase for two-stage citrulline production in E. coli. Metabolic Engineering (2020). DOI:10.1016/j.ymben.2020.03.004. [PMC7225747]
growth_records.snippet: cells did not grow after removing arginine ... the doubly dysregulated strain had a biomass-specific citrulline production rate of 0.73 mmol gDW-1 h-1, which decreased to 0.25 mmol gDW-1 h-1 between 20 and 32 h. ... 60 mg/L
growth_records.notes: No growth after arginine removal (OD constant at 0.05), so growth_rate=0. Specific citrulline production 0.73 mmol/gDW/h first 10 h, decreasing to 0.25 mmol/gDW/h (20-32 h); production stopped 44-54 h. Final citrulline 60 mg/L (3-fold higher than delta-argG only, which reached 20 mg/L, and delta-argG delta-argR 40 mg/L). Two decreasing-time-interval rates emitted as separate secretion entries.

growth_records.strain: delta-argG complemented with temperature-sensitive ArgG variants (nine strains), arginine-supplemented
growth_records.cite: Schramm et al. High-throughput enrichment of temperature-sensitive argininosuccinate synthetase for two-stage citrulline production in E. coli. Metabolic Engineering (2020). DOI:10.1016/j.ymben.2020.03.004. [PMC7225747]
growth_records.snippet: Addition of arginine could fully restore growth at 42 C, with maximum growth rates of 0.70 +/- 0.02 h-1.
growth_records.notes: Maximum specific growth rate 0.70 +/- 0.02 h-1 (reported, plate-reader OD, n=3) when 1 mM arginine restores growth of the arginine-auxotrophic ts-ArgG strains at 42C. Doubling time = ln2/0.70 = 0.99 h. Pooled value across the nine strains.

up.method: DERIVED qS=mu_max/Yxs=0.66 h-1 / 0.50 gDW/g glc =1.32 g glc/gDW/h; 1.32/180.16 g/mol *1000 =7.33 mmol/gDW/h

yields.Yxs: 0.50 g biomass/g glucose (measured in batch cultivations of both non-transformed and plasmid-bearing E. coli LE392)

growth_records.cite: Popov, Mladen et al. Effects of a recombinant gene expression on ColE1-like plasmid segregation in Escherichia coli. BMC Biotechnology (2011). DOI:10.1186/1472-6750-11-18. [PMC3061898]
growth_records.method: reported (mu_max measured from batch growth kinetics on M9+glucose); doubling_time calculated td=ln2/0.66=1.050 h
growth_records.snippet: a parameter value of 0.66 h-1 was estimated from the growth kinetics of E. coli LE392 cells cultivated in minimal (M9) medium supplemented with glucose in a batch reactor ... By batch cultivations of both non-transformed and plasmid-bearing
growth_records.notes: mu_max=0.66 h-1 experimentally measured (host, plasmid-free). Yxs=0.50 gDW/g glucose measured in batch. qGlucose is DERIVED not measured: qS=mu/Yxs=0.66/0.50=1.32 g glc/gDW/h; convert with glucose MW 180.16 g/mol: 1.32/180.16*1000=7.33 mmol/gDW/h. Glucose feed/inlet sF=1.96 g/L. Ks=0.005 g/L cited from Atkinson et al (not measured here).

up.method: DERIVED qS=D/Yxs=0.3 h-1 / 0.50 gDW/g =0.60 g glc/gDW/h; 0.60/180.16*1000=3.33 mmol/gDW/h (uses batch Yxs; authors note chemostat Yxs decreases with D)

sec.method: measured by ELISA in cell lysate (concentration p reported graphically, Fig 3); not expressed as a specific rate

yields.Yxs: 0.50 g biomass/g glucose (batch value; chemostat Yxs lower and D-dependent, not numerically reported)

growth_records.cite: Popov, Mladen et al. Effects of a recombinant gene expression on ColE1-like plasmid segregation in Escherichia coli. BMC Biotechnology (2011). DOI:10.1186/1472-6750-11-18. [PMC3061898]
growth_records.method: calculated (steady-state chemostat: mu=D=0.3 h-1); doubling_time td=ln2/0.3=2.31 h
growth_records.snippet: Batch and chemostat cultivations were performed in M9 medium containing 1.96 g/L glucose (without antibiotic) ... Feeding of chemostat cultivations was initiated after the initial batch phase (cell density of A600=1.5-1.6) at a constant dil
growth_records.notes: Steady-state chemostat: mu=D=0.3 h-1 (not independently measured). qGlucose DERIVED: qS=D/Yxs=0.3/0.50=0.60 g glc/gDW/h =3.33 mmol/gDW/h using MW 180.16; uses BATCH Yxs=0.50, and authors explicitly warn chemostat Yxs decreases with dilution rate, so this qS is an upper-bound approximation, not experimental. Residual glucose s measured during chemostat (Fig 3) but not tabulated. Plasmid copy numbers Np 41/24/30/22 (qPCR). Growth condition (D=0.3, medium) identical across the four plasmid strains; single record covers them.

growth_records.cite: Nguyen J et al. A distinct growth physiology enhances bacterial growth under rapid nutrient fluctuations. Nature Communications (2021). DOI:10.1038/s41467-021-23439-8. [PMC8209047]
growth_records.snippet: G_low and G_high (1.07 +/- 0.23 and 2.86 +/- 0.14 h-1, respectively) ... The corresponding value of G_ave was 2.31 +/- 0.18 h-1. C_high = 2% LB, C_low = 0.1% LB, C_ave = 1.05% LB.
growth_records.notes: Steady-state control at C_high = 2% LB. Reported G_high = 2.86 +/- 0.14 h-1 (single-cell mean of instantaneous growth rates >3 h; also stabilized value G_high = 2.84 +/- 0.08 h-1). IMPORTANT: growth rate G here is defined as VOLUME-DOUBLING rate (base-2) from V(t+dt)=V(t)*2^(G*dt), i.e. doublings per hour, NOT the natural-log exponential rate constant. Therefore doubling_time = 1/G = 1/2.86 = 0.35 h; equivalent exponential mu = G*ln2 = 2.86*0.6931 = 1.98 h-1. No substrate uptake or product secretion rates were measured (metabolomic profiling identified serine as likely preferred metabolite but reported no quantitative uptake rates). Temperature 37 C (LIS incubator, Methods). pH not stated.

growth_records.cite: Nguyen J et al. A distinct growth physiology enhances bacterial growth under rapid nutrient fluctuations. Nature Communications (2021). DOI:10.1038/s41467-021-23439-8. [PMC8209047]
growth_records.snippet: The corresponding value of G_ave was 2.31 +/- 0.18 h-1 (n = 13 replicate experiments). C_ave = (C_high + C_low)/2 (i.e., 1.05% LB).
growth_records.notes: Steady-state control at C_ave = 1.05% LB. G_ave = 2.31 +/- 0.18 h-1 (mean of single-cell growth rates after 3 h, n=13). G is base-2 volume-doubling rate (doublings/h), so doubling_time = 1/2.31 = 0.43 h; exponential mu = 2.31*ln2 = 1.60 h-1. No uptake/secretion rates measured. Temperature 37 C. pH not stated.

growth_records.cite: Nguyen J et al. A distinct growth physiology enhances bacterial growth under rapid nutrient fluctuations. Nature Communications (2021). DOI:10.1038/s41467-021-23439-8. [PMC8209047]
growth_records.snippet: cells that fluctuates between growth at two steady states, G_low and G_high (1.07 +/- 0.23 and 2.86 +/- 0.14 h-1, respectively). C_low = 0.1% LB.
growth_records.notes: Steady-state control at C_low = 0.1% LB. G_low = 1.07 +/- 0.23 h-1 (single-cell mean; steady G_low ~0.96 h-1 in some replicate). G is base-2 volume-doubling rate (doublings/h), so doubling_time = 1/1.07 = 0.93 h; exponential mu = 1.07*ln2 = 0.74 h-1. No uptake/secretion rates measured. Temperature 37 C. pH not stated. Additional fluctuating-nutrient conditions (30 s, 5, 15, 60 min oscillations between C_low and C_high) gave mean G_fluc of 1.93+/-0.16, 1.53+/-0.20, 1.15+/-0.28, 1.15+/-0.13 h-1 respectively (not emitted as separate steady records because they are time-averaged over an oscillating environment).

up.method: initial glycerol 2.76 g/L fully depleted by ~6 h (DO rise); 2.76/6 = 0.46 g/L/h

yields.Yxs_model_fitted: Y11 (biomass/glycerol) = 0.440; Y12 (biomass on S2 in combination 1) = 0.881; Y22 (biomass on S2 in combination 2) = 0.254 (cybernetic-model-fitted values, Table 2)

growth_records.cite: Ganjave SD et al. High cell density cultivation of E. coli in shake flasks for the production of recombinant proteins. Biotechnology Reports (Amsterdam) (2022). DOI:10.1016/j.btre.2021.e00694. [PMC8718739]
growth_records.snippet: the combination of SOB with glycerol yielded OD600 of 12-15 in the same period [6 h]. A continuous decrease in DO was observed for the first 6 h, followed by a sharp rise concomitant with the depletion of glycerol. an initial glycerol conce
growth_records.notes: Batch phase, recombinant E. coli BL21(DE3) in SOB+glycerol, 32 C, 200 rpm, aerobic shake flask, initial OD600 0.05. growth_rate CALCULATED as exponential mu from OD600: mu = ln(OD/OD0)/t = ln(13.5/0.05)/6 h = ln(270)/6 = 5.598/6 = 0.93 h-1 (using OD600 midpoint 13.5 of the reported 12-15 range at 6 h); range 0.91-0.95 h-1 (OD 12 -> 0.91, OD 15 -> 0.95). doubling_time = ln2/mu = 0.74 h. Glycerol uptake computed volumetrically only: 2.76 g/L consumed over 6 h = 0.46 g/L/h; cannot convert to mmol/gDW/h because gDW at 6 h is not reported (DCW measured only at end of cultivation). Yield coefficients Y11=0.440, Y12=0.881, Y22=0.254 are cybernetic-model-fitted (Table 2), not directly measured. Fitted maximum specific growth rate mu_max for combination 1 (glycerol+complex-N+O2) = 1.497 h-1 (model parameter, not a measured rate).

growth_records.cite: Ganjave SD et al. High cell density cultivation of E. coli in shake flasks for the production of recombinant proteins. Biotechnology Reports (Amsterdam) (2022). DOI:10.1016/j.btre.2021.e00694. [PMC8718739]
growth_records.snippet: LB or SOB alone yielded OD600 of 3-4, in 6 h.
growth_records.notes: Batch on complex medium (LB or SOB) WITHOUT added glycerol, 32 C, 200 rpm, aerobic. growth_rate CALCULATED from OD600: mu = ln(3.5/0.05)/6 h = ln(70)/6 = 4.248/6 = 0.71 h-1 (OD600 midpoint 3.5 of reported 3-4 range); range 0.68-0.73 h-1 (OD 3 -> 0.68, OD 4 -> 0.73). doubling_time = ln2/0.71 = 0.98 h. Paper lumps LB-alone and SOB-alone together (both OD600 3-4 at 6 h); LB composition given in medium_composition (SOB alternative: tryptone 20, yeast extract 5, NaCl 0.5, KCl 0.186 g/L). No uptake/secretion rates measured on these carbon-source-limited complex media.

growth_records.medium: SOB+glycerol batch then glycerol+yeast-extract fed-batch (shake flask HCD)

up.units: mL/h of 25% w/v glycerol+50% w/v yeast extract feed (0.5-0.78 mL/h across experiments)
up.method: syringe-pump feed rate, Table 1

yields.final_biomass: 19.9-21.5 g DCW/L (24 h); optimized run 20.7 g DCW/L; model predicted 19.44 g DCW/L

product_yields.note: volumetric protein productivity increased 8-34-fold vs batch (protein-specific)

growth_records.cite: Ganjave SD et al. High cell density cultivation of E. coli in shake flasks for the production of recombinant proteins. Biotechnology Reports (Amsterdam) (2022). DOI:10.1016/j.btre.2021.e00694. [PMC8718739]
growth_records.snippet: The biomass concentrations achieved in 24 h were 19.9-21.5 g DCW/L, in agreement with the model predicted value of 19.44 g DCW/L. The optimized process yielded 20.7 g dry cell weight/L.
growth_records.notes: Fed-batch HCD, recombinant E. coli BL21(DE3), 32 C, 200 rpm, aerobic shake flask. Primary measured outcome = final biomass 19.9-21.5 g DCW/L (optimized 20.7 g DCW/L) at 24 h (DCW measured in triplicate, dried at 95 C to constant weight). growth_rate not cleanly definable (fed-batch, DO-limited); model-fitted mu_max(combination 1)=1.497 h-1 (Table 2, not measured). Feed compositions/rates from Table 1 (glycerol 20-30% w/v, yeast extract 30-50% w/v, 0.5-0.78 mL/h). Yield coefficient Y11=0.440 is cybernetic-model-fitted. Residual glycerol measured by enzymatic assay (Megazyme). No excreted-product (acetate etc.) rates reported.

growth_records.medium: SOB+glycerol batch then glycerol+yeast-extract fed-batch (3 L bioreactor)

up.units: mL/h of 17.5% w/v glycerol + 24.5% w/v yeast extract feed
up.method: continuous feed rate stated in Methods

product_yields.note: ~2.8-fold further increase in eYFP volumetric productivity vs the shake-flask fed-batch

growth_records.cite: Ganjave SD et al. High cell density cultivation of E. coli in shake flasks for the production of recombinant proteins. Biotechnology Reports (Amsterdam) (2022). DOI:10.1016/j.btre.2021.e00694. [PMC8718739]
growth_records.snippet: Fed-batch cultivation for one of the representative proteins, eYFP was performed in 3 L capacity Applikon bioreactor at 32 C, with an agitation rate of 615 rpm and aeration rate of 1 vvm. The nutrient mixture containing 17.5% glycerol and 2
growth_records.notes: Scale-up fed-batch of the optimized recipe in a 3 L bioreactor, E. coli BL21(DE3)-eYFP, 32 C, 615 rpm, 1 vvm aerobic. Feed 17.5% w/v glycerol + 24.5% w/v yeast extract at 5.4 mL/h from 6 h; IPTG at 12 h; 24 h total. Reported outcome = 2.8-fold higher eYFP volumetric productivity vs shake-flask fed-batch (final DCW for bioreactor run not separately tabulated in main text). growth_rate not directly reported; no substrate uptake per gDW or secretion rates reported (only volumetric feed rate). pH not stated (controlled bioreactor but setpoint not given in extracted text).

growth_records.strain: photophilic strain bJAG132 (BW25113 host; chromosomal mVenus, pSC101 Opto-T7 pAB276, p15A CAT-under-T7 mJAG063)
growth_records.cite: Gutiérrez Mena, Joaquín et al. Dynamic cybergenetic control of bacterial co-culture composition via optogenetic feedback. Nature Communications (2022). DOI:10.1038/s41467-022-32392-z. [PMC9381578]
growth_records.snippet: When grown in the presence of 10.5 uM chloramphenicol, we can precisely set the growth rate of the strain in a range between ~1 h-1 and ~1.9h-1 by modulating the intensity of input light.
growth_records.notes: Photophilic strain bJAG132 (E. coli BW25113) in 10.5 uM chloramphenicol; minimum steady-state growth ~1.0 h-1 in the dark (low CAT expression). doubling_time = ln2/1.0 = 0.69 h. Growth rate measured by eVOLVER turbidostat OD600. Values are approximate (~1 h-1) as stated in text (Fig. 2b). No substrate uptake/secretion measured.

growth_records.strain: photophilic strain bJAG132 (BW25113 host; chromosomal mVenus, pSC101 Opto-T7 pAB276, p15A CAT-under-T7 mJAG063)
growth_records.cite: Gutiérrez Mena, Joaquín et al. Dynamic cybergenetic control of bacterial co-culture composition via optogenetic feedback. Nature Communications (2022). DOI:10.1038/s41467-022-32392-z. [PMC9381578]
growth_records.snippet: we can precisely set the growth rate of the strain in a range between ~1 h-1 and ~1.9h-1 by modulating the intensity of input light.
growth_records.notes: Photophilic strain bJAG132 in 10.5 uM chloramphenicol under maximum blue light; maximum steady-state growth ~1.9 h-1. doubling_time = ln2/1.9 = 0.36 h. Measured by eVOLVER turbidostat OD600 (Fig. 2b,d). Constitutive strains bJAG234/bJAG235/bJAG236 growth rates shown only graphically in Fig. 2d (no numeric values in text); bJAG236 grows faster than photophilic max, bJAG235 lies between the photophilic extremes and was chosen as the constitutive partner. No uptake/secretion/MFA data in this paper.

growth_records.cite: Cermak, Nathan et al. High-throughput measurement of single-cell growth rates using serial microfluidic mass sensor arrays. Nature Biotechnology (2016). DOI:10.1038/nbt.3666. [PMC5064867]
growth_records.snippet: In rich media (LB) at 37 C, we found that the average growth rate of individual bacteria was 2.14 +/- 0.02/h (mean +/- standard error, 19.4 minute doubling time) with a coefficient of variation of 7.4% excluding four outlying small cells (F
growth_records.notes: Single-cell exponential growth rate (mass accumulation rate per unit mass) measured directly by serial SMR array. mu = 2.14 +/- 0.02 /h reported. Doubling time reported as 19.4 min = 0.323 h; check ln2/2.14 = 0.3239 h = 19.4 min (consistent). CV of growth rate 7.4% (four outlying small cells excluded). No substrate-uptake, product-secretion, MFA flux, or yield data reported (method/instrumentation paper). E. coli grown overnight in LB broth (Miller, Difco) at 37C, diluted 10,000-fold in LB + 0.2% Tween-80 (Tween added to prevent sticking to device walls), incubated 1.5-2.5 h at 37C before loading. Base LB Miller composition not stated in paper -> medium_composition_stated false. pH not stated.
